topics.title: Definition of a Product
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT862 - ELECTRONIC PRODUCT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definition of a Product

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Product** is any tangible good, service, idea, or a combination thereof that a manufacturer or service provider offers to a consumer to satisfy a specific need, want, or demand. According to **Philip Kotler**, a product is *anything that can be offered to a market for attention, acquisition, use, or consumption that might satisfy a want or need*.

In the specific context of **Electronic Product Design**, a product is defined as a **physical, electro-mechanical, or software-integrated system** that incorporates electronic components (active devices like transistors, ICs; passive devices like resistors, capacitors; embedded firmware; and electromechanical actuators/sensors) to perform a **defined functional objective** for a clearly identified target user or market segment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Must Memorize):**
> *An electronic product is a customer-centric engineered system that converts electrical energy into a useful functional output — physical, informational, or computational — to deliver a defined value proposition to its end-user across its operational life cycle.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Think of a product as a **"layered onion"** rather than a single object:

- Imagine you buy a **smart fitness watch** (e.g., a basic wristband with a heart-rate sensor).
- On the **outside**, you see a sleek black band with a small display.
- But what you **really bought** is the ability to monitor your health, get notifications, and track your sleep.
- The watch is just the **carrier** of the underlying value: *peace of mind about your health*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> A product is **not** just the physical box you receive. It is the **complete bundle of utility, experience, support, and brand promise** that comes with that box. In Electronic Product Design, we engineer every layer of this "onion" — from the silicon die inside the chip to the unboxing experience on your doorstep.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

The following industry-standard metrics are universally applied to characterize a product's design maturity and commercial viability:

- **Time-to-Market (TTM):** Typically **6 to 24 months** for consumer electronics.
- **Product Life Cycle (PLC):** Average **2 to 5 years** for consumer electronics, **5 to 15 years** for industrial electronics.
- **Bill of Materials (BOM) Cost Target:** Generally **25% to 40%** of the retail price for mass-market consumer electronics.
- **Return Merchandise Authorization (RMA) Rate:** Industry-acceptable threshold is **less than 2%** in the first 90 days.
- **First Pass Yield (FPY) Target in Manufacturing:** Minimum **95%** for a commercially viable electronic product.
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** Minimum **50,000 hours** for high-reliability industrial products.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 of PEERT862 lays the *philosophical and terminological foundation* upon which Modules 2 through 5 (Design Process, Prototyping, Testing, and Manufacturing) are built. Mastering this definition is **non-negotiable** for higher-mark answers in subsequent modules.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Summary Sheet

### 2.1 Characteristics of a Product (Operational Logic Steps)

A product is fully characterized by the following logical attributes. Each step answers a specific engineering or commercial question:

1. **Tangibility / Intangibility** — Is the product physical (e.g., a smartphone), purely digital (e.g., a firmware update), or a hybrid (e.g., a smart-home subscription bundle)?
2. **Utility / Function** — What specific user problem does it solve? (e.g., a Bluetooth speaker delivers *portable audio entertainment*).
3. **Quality and Reliability** — How well does it perform its function consistently over time? (Quantified by **MTBF**).
4. **Design and Aesthetics** — How does it *look, feel, and interact* with the user? (Industrial design dimension).
5. **Branding and Packaging** — What identity does the user associate with it? (Marketing dimension).
6. **Lifecycle and Sustainability** — How is it produced, used, recycled, and disposed of?
7. **Value Proposition** — What is the *trade-off* the user accepts between cost and benefit?

### 2.2 Kotler's Five Levels of a Product (Core KTU High-Yield Framework)

This is the **single most important framework** for KTU ESE questions on this topic. The product is seen as **five concentric layers**, each adding value:

| Level # | Layer Name | Engineering / Commercial Meaning | Electronic Product Example (Smart Speaker) |
|:-------:|:-----------|:----------------------------------|:--------------------------------------------|
| **1** | **Core Benefit** | The fundamental need the product satisfies | *Access to hands-free, voice-activated information* |
| **2** | **Basic Product** | The minimal physical/digital manifestation | *The speaker hardware with microphone, speaker driver, and basic plastic enclosure* |
| **3** | **Expected Product** | The set of features the user *assumes* are standard | *Wi-Fi connectivity, noise cancellation, decent audio quality, voice assistant integration* |
| **4** | **Augmented Product** | The differentiation layer — features beyond expectation | *Multi-room audio sync, premium aluminum finish, free cloud storage for voice history, 2-year warranty* |
| **5** | **Potential Product** | The futuristic evolution and upgradability | *AI-driven predictive responses, integration with smart home ecosystem, future firmware-defined capabilities* |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in KTU Exams:** Examiners frequently award 7 marks just for diagrammatically explaining these five levels. Always draw the **onion/concentric layer diagram** when answering.

### 2.3 Classification of Products (KTU High-Yield Classification Table)

| Classification Dimension | Sub-Types | Engineering Implication |
|:------------------------|:----------|:------------------------|
| **By Tangibility** | Tangible Goods / Intangible Services / Hybrid | Defines whether PCB-level hardware design is the primary deliverable |
| **By Durability** | Durable (TV, Refrigerator) / Non-Durable (Battery, Consumable Sensor) | Determines warranty period and after-sales service model |
| **By User Type** | Consumer Electronics / Industrial Electronics / Medical Electronics / Automotive Electronics | Drives regulatory compliance (BIS, CE, FCC, AAR) |
| **By Kotler's Convenience Theory** | Convenience Products / Shopping Products / Specialty Products / Unsought Products | Defines the marketing channel and pricing strategy |
| **By Technology Generation** | First-Generation (analog) / Second-Generation (digital) / Third-Generation (smart) / Fourth-Generation (AI/IoT) | Defines the supply chain and component sourcing strategy |

### 2.4 Product vs. Service — A Critical KTU Distinction

| Attribute | Product | Service |
|:----------|:--------|:--------|
| **Tangibility** | Physical and measurable | Intangible and experiential |
| **Storability** | Can be inventoried | Cannot be stored |
| **Variability** | Standardized across units | Highly variable per interaction |
| **Transfer of Ownership** | Ownership is transferred | No ownership transfer — right to use is granted |
| **Engineering Focus** | Hardware design + firmware + manufacturing | Process design + customer journey + SLA |
| **Example** | A sold Wi-Fi router | A 1-year internet subscription |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electronic Product Design (KTU Focus):** Most modern electronic products are **Product-Service Hybrids**. For example, a *smartphone* is a product, but the *iCloud subscription* tied to it is a service. The designer must architect **both layers simultaneously**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **For Hardware Engineers:** Defines the BOM, enclosure design, thermal dissipation, and PCB constraints.
- **For Embedded Software Engineers:** Defines the firmware scope, OTA upgrade pathways, and API surface.
- **For Industrial Designers:** Defines the ergonomics, color palette, and material selection.
- **For Marketing Teams:** Defines the *augmented layer* — packaging, branding, after-sales experience.
- **For Compliance Engineers:** Defines the regulatory pathway (BIS, RoHS, CE, FCC, WEEE).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Conceptual Derivation

Since this is a foundational, conceptual topic within an Engineering Design course, the analytical work consists of **structured feature-by-feature analytical matrices** rather than algebraic derivations. The following exhaustive analyses are aligned with the KTU valuation scheme and will earn full marks when presented clearly.

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix 1: Kotler's Five Levels — Applied Derivation for a Smart Wearable Fitness Band

**Step 1 — Identify the Core Benefit:**
The fundamental human need being satisfied is *the need to know one's health status in real time without visiting a clinic*.

**Step 2 — Define the Basic Product:**
The minimum hardware required is a *wristband with a microcontroller, optical heart-rate sensor, vibration motor, and basic OLED display*.

**Step 3 — Specify the Expected Product:**
The user *expects* a companion mobile app, 5-day battery life, water resistance up to 50 meters, and Bluetooth sync.

**Step 4 — Engineer the Augmented Product:**
Differentiation is achieved through *premium silicone straps, AMOLED always-on display, ECG sensor, SpO2 monitor, 14-day battery, sleep AI, and 2-year warranty*.

**Step 5 — Envision the Potential Product:**
Future scope includes *non-invasive glucose monitoring, AI-based cardiac anomaly prediction, integration with hospital EHR systems, and modular add-on sensors*.

$$
\text{Value}_{\text{Total}} = \text{Layer}_1 + \text{Layer}_2 + \text{Layer}_3 + \text{Layer}_4 + \text{Layer}_5
$$

$$
\text{Value}_{\text{Total}} = \text{Core} + \text{Basic} + \text{Expected} + \text{Augmented} + \text{Potential}
$$

Where each layer adds a **multiplicative** commercial premium over the previous layer — i.e., a well-augmented product can command a **40% to 200%** price premium over a basic equivalent.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix 2: Product vs. Service — Step-by-Step Decision Tree Analysis

To determine whether a deliverable is a product, a service, or a hybrid, the following exhaustive decision tree is applied:

**Step 1 — Assess Tangibility:**
Is there a physical, deliverable hardware unit? If **Yes → Product**. If **No** and only a process is delivered → **Service**.

**Step 2 — Assess Ownership Transfer:**
Does the customer take ownership of a physical asset? If **Yes → Product-led**. If **No**, but right to use is granted → **Service-led**.

**Step 3 — Assess Repeatability:**
Is the output identical across units? If **Yes → Standardized Product**. If **No → Customized Service**.

**Step 4 — Assess Revenue Model:**
Is it a **one-time sale** or a **recurring subscription**?

**Step 5 — Final Classification Matrix:**

| Use Case | Product Component | Service Component | Final Classification |
|:---------|:------------------|:------------------|:---------------------|
| **A standalone Bluetooth speaker sold in a box** | 100% | 0% | Pure Product |
| **A 1-year cloud storage subscription** | 0% | 100% | Pure Service |
| **A smartphone sold with a 2-year warranty** | 90% | 10% | Product-Dominant Hybrid |
| **A Netflix subscription bundled free with a smart TV** | 60% | 40% | Balanced Hybrid |
| **A monthly drone-as-a-service (DaaS) for agricultural survey** | 30% | 70% | Service-Dominant Hybrid |

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix 3: Exhaustive Classification of Electronic Products

| Sector | Typical Product | Core User | Regulatory Standard | Typical PLC |
|:-------|:----------------|:----------|:--------------------|:------------|
| **Consumer Electronics** | Smartphone, Smart TV, Earbuds | End consumer | BIS, CE, FCC | 2 to 4 years |
| **Industrial Electronics** | PLC, SCADA, VFD | Factory automation engineer | IEC, UL | 10 to 20 years |
| **Medical Electronics** | Patient Monitor, MRI, Ventilator | Clinician + Patient | FDA, ISO 13485, CE-MDR | 7 to 15 years |
| **Automotive Electronics** | ECU, ADAS, Infotainment | Driver + OEM | AEC-Q100, ISO 26262 | 5 to 10 years |
| **Aerospace & Defense** | Avionics, Radar, Drones | Military / Airline | DO-254, MIL-STD-810 | 15 to 30 years |
| **IoT & Wearables** | Fitness band, Smart Home Hub | End consumer | BIS, RoHS, WPC | 2 to 3 years |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Strategy:** When a 14-mark question asks you to "explain the classification of electronic products," present this matrix in a clean table and then expand on **any two sectors** in 3 to 4 lines each. This structured approach consistently secures **12 to 14 marks** in KTU ESE.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1: Kotler's Five Levels of a Product (Concentric Layer Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L5["LEVEL 5 POTENTIAL PRODUCT Future evolution and AI upgrades"]:::outer
    L4["LEVEL 4 AUGMENTED PRODUCT Differentiation features beyond expectation"]:::mid
    L3["LEVEL 3 EXPECTED PRODUCT Standard features assumed by user"]:::mid
    L2["LEVEL 2 BASIC PRODUCT Minimal hardware manifestation"]:::core
    L1["LEVEL 1 CORE BENEFIT Fundamental user need satisfied"]:::core
    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1

    classDef outer fill:#1F4E79,stroke:#0B2A47,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef mid fill:#2E75B6,stroke:#1F4E79,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef core fill:#9DC3E6,stroke:#2E75B6,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B2A47
```

```mermaid
    B --> B2["Intangible Services"]
    B --> B3["Hybrid Offerings"]

    C --> C2["Non-Durable Goods"]
    D --> D1["Consumer Electronics"]
    D --> D2["Industrial Electronics"]
    D --> D3["Medical Electronics"]
    D --> D4["Automotive Electronics"]
    D --> D5["Aerospace and Defense"]
    E --> E1["Convenience Products"]
    E --> E2["Shopping Products"]
    E --> E3["Specialty Products"]
    E --> E4["Unsought Products"]

    classDef root fill:#C00000,stroke:#7F0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef branch fill:#FFC000,stroke:#BF8F00,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B2A47
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3: Product vs. Service — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start["CUSTOMER NEED"]:::start
    P["PRODUCT DOMAIN"]:::product
    S["SERVICE DOMAIN"]:::service
    H["HYBRID DOMAIN"]:::hybrid
    PD["Physical deliverable Hardware ownership One-time revenue"]:::pdesc

    Start --> P
    Start --> S
    Start --> H
    P --> PD
    S --> SD
    H --> HD
    PD --> OutP["OUTCOME Tangible asset in hand"]:::outp
```

### 4.4 Diagram 4: Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Electronic Product (Mapping the Product Definition to Engineering Blocks)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Input["USER NEED AND MARKET GAP"]:::input
    C["CORE BENEFIT BLOCK Identify the fundamental utility"]:::core1
    B["BASIC PRODUCT BLOCK PCB, Microcontroller, Sensors, Actuators, Power Supply"]:::core2
    E["EXPECTED PRODUCT BLOCK Connectivity, Standard Compliance, Basic UI"]:::core3
    A["AUGMENTED PRODUCT BLOCK Premium Finish, Warranty, AI Features, Cloud Sync"]:::core4
    P["POTENTIAL PRODUCT BLOCK OTA Upgrade Path, Modular Expansion, Future APIs"]:::core5

    Input --> C --> B --> E --> A --> P --> Output

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a product. With a suitable example, explain the concept of a "hybrid product" in the context of electronic product design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **product** is any tangible good, service, idea, or combination thereof that is offered to a market to satisfy a specific customer need or want (definition: **1 mark**).

A **hybrid product** is an offering that combines a physical hardware component with an ongoing service layer, sold as a single integrated value proposition to the customer (concept: **1 mark**).

**Example:** A **smart speaker** (Amazon Echo) is a hybrid product. The physical device (hardware) is sold once, but the **Alexa cloud service, music streaming subscription, and smart-home control subscription** are ongoing service components bundled with the hardware (example: **1 mark**).

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between the "core product" and the "augmented product" with reference to a smartphone.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **core product** is the fundamental benefit the customer is actually buying — the underlying need being satisfied (**1 mark**).

The **augmented product** is the set of additional features, services, and brand experiences that go **beyond** the customer's basic expectations and provide competitive differentiation (**1 mark**).

**Smartphone example:**

- **Core product:** The ability to *communicate, access information, and stay connected* from anywhere.
- **Augmented product:** Premium titanium frame, 200MP camera with AI imaging, 5-year software update commitment, complimentary 1-year cloud storage, and 24x7 priority customer support (**1 mark**).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** A student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) for **7 marks** and (b) for **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain **Kotler's Five Levels of a Product** in detail. Use a **smart wearable fitness band** as a running example to illustrate each level. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **classification of electronic products** based on **user type, durability, and tangibility**. Provide at least **two examples** for each sub-class. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Kotler's Five Levels (7 marks)

A product is not a single entity but a **five-layered structure** as proposed by marketing guru **Philip Kotler**. Each layer adds incremental value to the customer. The five levels, applied to a **smart wearable fitness band**, are:

1. **Level 1 — Core Benefit (1 mark):**
   The fundamental human need being satisfied is *the need to monitor personal health metrics (heart rate, sleep, activity) in real time without visiting a clinic*. The user is not really buying a wristband; they are buying *peace of mind about their health*.

2. **Level 2 — Basic Product (1 mark):**
   The minimal physical realization is a *silicone wristband housing a microcontroller (e.g., ARM Cortex-M4), an optical PPG heart-rate sensor, a 3-axis accelerometer, a small monochrome OLED display, and a lithium-polymer battery*. This is the *baseline* form factor.

3. **Level 3 — Expected Product (1.5 marks):**
   The user *assumes* the following features will be present: Bluetooth 5.0 sync with a smartphone, 5-day battery life, IP68 water resistance, a companion mobile app (Android + iOS), and basic step/calorie tracking. Failure to deliver any of these results in customer dissatisfaction.

4. **Level 4 — Augmented Product (1.5 marks):**
   This is the **differentiation layer**. Premium fitness bands add: AMOLED always-on display, ECG and SpO2 sensors, 14-day battery, AI-driven sleep coaching, premium aluminum finish, interchangeable straps, free cloud data backup, and a 2-year warranty. These features command a **40% to 100%** price premium over basic equivalents.

5. **Level 5 — Potential Product (1 mark):**
   The future evolution: non-invasive glucose monitoring, AI-based cardiac anomaly detection with doctor alerts, integration with hospital EHR systems, and modular add-on sensors (GPS, temperature, blood pressure cuff). This layer represents the *upgradability roadmap* and protects the product from obsolescence.

**Concluding Line (0.5 mark):** A well-designed electronic product is engineered across all five levels simultaneously; a product that addresses only Levels 1 and 2 is a *commodity*, while a product that excels at Levels 4 and 5 is a *brand*.

#### Part (b) — Classification of Electronic Products (7 marks)

Electronic products can be systematically classified along three primary axes:

**Axis 1: By User Type (3 marks)**

- **Consumer Electronics:** Smartphones, smart TVs, wireless earbuds, gaming consoles. Target: end consumer. Regulatory standards: BIS, CE, FCC. PLC: 2 to 4 years. *Examples: Apple iPhone, Sony Bravia TV.*
- **Industrial Electronics:** PLCs, motor drives, SCADA systems, industrial sensors. Target: factory automation engineers. Regulatory standards: IEC, UL. PLC: 10 to 20 years. *Examples: Siemens SIMATIC PLC, ABB VFD.*
- **Medical Electronics:** Patient monitors, MRI machines, ventilators, insulin pumps. Target: clinicians and patients. Regulatory standards: FDA, ISO 13485, CE-MDR. PLC: 7 to 15 years. *Examples: Philips IntelliVue monitor, Medtronic pacemaker.*

**Axis 2: By Durability (2 marks)**

- **Durable Electronic Products:** Television, refrigerator, washing machine, laptop. Designed for **multi-year use**, with warranty periods of 1 to 5 years. *Example: LG 55-inch OLED TV (8+ years of expected use).*
- **Non-Durable Electronic Products:** Batteries, disposable sensors, ink cartridges, single-use medical electrodes. Designed for **single or short-term use**. *Example: Duracell AA battery (1 to 2 years), Medtronic glucose test strips (single use).*

**Axis 3: By Tangibility (2 marks)**

- **Tangible Electronic Products:** Physical hardware devices that the user can hold — PCB, enclosure, display, connectors. *Example: A Bosch power drill.*
- **Intangible Electronic Products:** Pure software/firmware deliverables — operating system updates, cloud services, OTA firmware, SaaS platforms. *Example: Microsoft 365 subscription, AWS IoT Core.*
- **Hybrid Electronic Products:** Hardware + service bundle. *Example: Apple iPhone + iCloud + AppleCare+ sold as one offering.*

**Concluding Line (0.5 mark):** A single electronic product can belong to **multiple classification axes simultaneously** — a smart insulin pump is *medical*, *durable*, and *hybrid* all at once.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **core vs. augmented product** concept. How does this concept influence the **design and marketing strategy** of an electronic product? Give **two real-world examples**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Distinguish clearly between a **product** and a **service**. Discuss the **Product-Service Hybrid (PSH)** model with suitable examples from the consumer electronics industry. Explain why PSH is becoming the **dominant business model** in modern electronic product design. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Core vs. Augmented Product (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** The **core product** is the fundamental benefit or utility that the customer is essentially buying. The **augmented product** is the additional layer of features, services, branding, and experience that goes beyond the customer's basic expectations and provides competitive differentiation.

**Diagram (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Core Product} \subset \text{Basic Product} \subset \text{Expected Product} \subset \text{Augmented Product} \subset \text{Potential Product}
$$

(Student should draw Kotler's 5-level onion diagram with annotated examples.)

**Influence on Design and Marketing Strategy (2 marks):**

- The **core product** drives the **engineering and R&D** investment — what technical problem are we solving?
- The **augmented product** drives the **industrial design, marketing budget, after-sales service, and brand positioning** — how do we differentiate and delight the user?

**Two Real-World Examples (1 mark):**

1. **Apple iPhone 15 Pro Max:** Core = *communication + computing on the go*. Augmented = *titanium body, A17 Pro chip, ProMotion display, 5-year iOS updates, AppleCare+ support.*
2. **Tesla Model 3:** Core = *electric mobility for daily commute*. Augmented = *OTA software updates, Autopilot, Supercharger network access, premium interior, mobile app control.*

#### Part (b) — Product vs. Service and Product-Service Hybrid (7 marks)

**Product vs. Service — Distinction Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Product | Service |
|:----------|:--------|:--------|
| Tangibility | Physical and measurable | Intangible and experiential |
| Storability | Can be inventoried in warehouses | Cannot be stored |
| Ownership | Transferred to the customer | No ownership transfer |
| Variability | Standardized per unit | Highly variable per delivery |
| Revenue Model | One-time sale | Recurring / subscription |
| Example | A sold smart TV | A Netflix subscription |

**Product-Service Hybrid (PSH) Model (2.5 marks):**

A **Product-Service Hybrid** is a business model in which a physical hardware product is sold bundled with one or more recurring service components (cloud subscription, warranty, maintenance, content, AI features) to create a **sticky, long-term customer relationship** and a **predictable recurring revenue stream**.

**Consumer Electronics Examples (1.5 marks):**

1. **Apple iPhone + iCloud + Apple TV+ + AppleCare+:** The iPhone is the product; the services (iCloud storage, Apple TV+ content, AppleCare+ extended warranty) form a hybrid bundle.
2. **Amazon Kindle + Kindle Unlimited + Audible Subscription:** The Kindle e-reader is the product; the book/audiobook subscription is the service.
3. **Tesla Model 3 + FSD (Full Self-Driving) Subscription + Premium Connectivity:** The car is the product; the AI feature subscription is the service.

**Why PSH is Dominant Today (1 mark):**

- Recurring revenue is **more predictable and higher-margin** than one-time hardware sales.
- It creates **customer lock-in** and reduces churn.
- It enables **continuous value delivery** via OTA upgrades, keeping the product relevant longer.
- It aligns the manufacturer's incentive with the customer's long-term satisfaction.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic — Avoid These!**
>
> 1. **Defining a product as *only* a physical object.** This is the **single biggest mistake**. Always mention that a product can be a service, idea, or hybrid. (Penalty: 1 to 2 marks lost in 3-mark questions, up to 3 marks in 14-mark questions.)
>
> 2. **Confusing "Expected" with "Augmented" levels.** Expected = *what the user assumes is standard*. Augmented = *what goes beyond expectation*. Mixing these up is a classic KTU pitfall. (Penalty: 1 mark per level mistakenly defined.)
>
> 3. **Writing generic, non-electronic examples** (e.g., soap, soap, shampoo) when the course is **Electronic Product Design**. Always anchor your examples in *electronic products* — smartphones, IoT, medical devices, automotive electronics. (Penalty: 1 to 2 marks for irrelevant examples.)
>
> 4. **Skipping the diagram.** A 14-mark question on Kotler's levels without the concentric/onion diagram will lose **2 to 3 marks** even if the textual answer is perfect. **Always draw the diagram.**
>
> 5. **Not writing the conclusion line.** A KTU answer without a concluding sentence loses the **final 0.5 mark** in the marking scheme. Always close with a crisp summary statement.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Definition of a Product**

- A **product** is anything offered to a market to satisfy a need or want — it can be **tangible, intangible, or hybrid**.
- The **Kotler's Five Levels** are: **Core Benefit, Basic Product, Expected Product, Augmented Product, Potential Product** — memorize the *exact order* and the *electronic product example* for each.
- **Core Product** = *the why* (fundamental benefit). **Augmented Product** = *the wow* (differentiator).
- Classification of products is **multi-axial**: tangibility, durability, user type, and Kotler's convenience theory — all apply *simultaneously*.
- **Modern electronic products are predominantly Product-Service Hybrids** (e.g., smartphone + cloud subscription).
- A complete electronic product specification must address **all five Kotler levels**, not just the hardware.
- The **TTM** for consumer electronics is **6 to 24 months**; **PLC** is **2 to 5 years**; **MTBF** must be **≥ 50,000 hours** for industrial products.
- Always use **electronic product examples** in your KTU answers — never generic FMCG or non-electronic examples.
- **Always draw the concentric diagram** when explaining Kotler's levels in a 14-mark answer.
- Always close your answer with a **concluding one-liner** to secure the final fractional mark.
- Module 1 definitions are the **foundation** for Modules 2 to 5 — do not skip or skim this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Product Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT862 - ELECTRONIC PRODUCT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Product Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Product Classification** is the systematic process of grouping products, services, and ideas into well-defined categories based on a common set of characteristics such as **durability, tangibility, consumer buying behavior, end-use application, and life cycle stage**. In the context of **Electronic Product Design (PEERT862)**, classification serves as the foundational step of the New Product Development (NPD) funnel, as it directly dictates the choice of design philosophy, manufacturing process, bill of materials (BoM), reliability targets, regulatory compliance pathway (BIS, CE, FCC), and cost engineering strategy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Product classification is a *gateway concept*. Every subsequent module — from Concept Generation (Module 2) to Prototyping & Testing (Module 5) — assumes the designer already knows *what class* of product is being designed. A mis-classification at this stage cascades into costly design rework later.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Terminology — "Product"**  
> A *product* is anything that can be offered to a **market** for attention, acquisition, use, or consumption that might satisfy a want or need. It includes physical objects (smartphone), services (cloud storage), persons (celebrity), places (tourism), organizations (IITs), ideas (Swachh Bharat), or experiences (VR gaming).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital Triage"

Imagine an accident & emergency ward in a hospital. The moment a patient arrives, the triage nurse classifies them into three buckets:

- 🟢 **Green (Minor)** — walk-in cold, routine checkup
- 🟡 **Yellow (Urgent)** — fracture, needs attention within hours
- 🔴 **Red (Critical)** — cardiac arrest, immediate ICU

This **classification** decides the *doctor assigned, the equipment used, the cost, the protocol, the recovery time* — everything.

In the exact same way, a **product manager or design engineer classifies an electronic product** the moment it enters the design pipeline. Is it a **throwaway consumer gadget** (Red Bull can — low cost, high volume)? A **durable white good** (LG refrigerator — high cost, long life)? Or a **high-reliability aerospace subsystem** (ISRO flight computer — extreme cost, ultra-high reliability)?

The classification dictates:
| Design Driver | Walkman (Disposable) | Microwave (Durable) | Pacemaker (Critical) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Target Life | **< 2 years** | **5–10 years** | **> 10 years** |
| MTBF Target | Low | Medium | **> 100,000 hrs** |
| Cost Sensitivity | Extreme | Moderate | Low |
| Compliance | Basic BIS | BIS + Energy Star | FDA + ISO 13485 |

---

### 1.3 The Three Master Classification Frameworks

In KTU's PEERT862, every product is classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

1. **Durability & Tangibility** (Philip Kotler's classical framework)
2. **Consumer vs. Industrial / B2C vs. B2B** (end-user framework)
3. **Life-Cycle Stage** (Introduction → Growth → Maturity → Decline)

We will study all three in detail in Section 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Framework A — Classification by Durability and Tangibility

Products are first split into two super-categories, then sub-divided:

#### A.1 Nondurable Goods (Disposable / FMCG-style Electronics)
- **Definition:** Tangible goods that are normally consumed in one or a few uses, OR have a useful life of **less than 1 year**.
- **Engineering Implication:** Designed for *low cost-per-unit, high volume, replaceable, not repairable.*
- **Electronic Examples:** AA alkaline batteries, single-use earphones, disposable e-cigarettes (vape pens), budget USB cables, promotional LED keychains.

#### A.2 Durable Goods
- **Definition:** Tangible goods that normally survive many uses — useful life of **1 year or more**.
- **Engineering Implication:** Higher reliability targets, repair-friendly design (modular BoM), warranty planning, after-sales service network.
- **Electronic Examples:** Smartphones, laptops, refrigerators, washing machines, televisions, inverters.

#### A.3 Services
- **Definition:** Intangible, inseparable, variable, perishable offerings.
- **Electronic Examples:** SaaS subscriptions (Adobe Creative Cloud), cloud storage (AWS S3), over-the-air (OTA) firmware updates, Netflix streaming.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in Classification**  
> - **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** Measured in **hours** or **FIT (Failures-In-Time, failures per $10^9$ device-hours)**.  
> - **Useful Life Benchmark:** $\geq \mathbf{1 \text{ year}}$ is the textbook cut-off for *durable*.  
> - **Annual Failure Rate (AFR) Threshold:** Consumer electronics typically target **AFR $\le$ 2%**; industrial $\le$ 0.5%; medical/aerospace $\le$ 0.01%.

---

### 2.2 Framework B — Consumer Products (Convenience, Shopping, Specialty, Unsought)

This is **Henry Assael's / Kotler's** four-tier classification based on **buyer behaviour and effort-to-purchase**:

#### B.1 Convenience Products
- **Buyer behaviour:** Routine, low-involvement, frequent purchase, minimal comparison.
- **Design focus:** *Shelf-availability, low price, brand visibility, packaging, point-of-sale displays.*
- **Electronic Examples:** Phone chargers, earbuds under ₹500, memory cards, batteries, mouse pads.

#### B.2 Shopping Products
- **Buyer behaviour:** High involvement, comparison-shopping, attribute-based evaluation (price, features, reviews).
- **Design focus:** *Differentiation, feature richness, aesthetic appeal, competitive benchmarking.*
- **Electronic Examples:** Smartphones, laptops, DSLR cameras, gaming consoles, smartwatches.

#### B.3 Specialty Products
- **Buyer behaviour:** Strong brand/preference loyalty, no substitutes acceptable, search effort high.
- **Design focus:** *Premium build, exclusive features, brand heritage, status signalling.*
- **Electronic Examples:** Apple iPhone Pro series, Bose noise-cancel headphones, Rolls-Royce of audio (Sennheiser Orpheus), Tesla Model S Plaid.

#### B.4 Unsought Products
- **Buyer behaviour:** Buyer does not know about it OR knows but does not want it.
- **Design focus:** *Aggressive marketing, demonstration, problem-solution framing.*
- **Electronic Examples:** Fire alarms (life insurance-like purchase), extended warranty plans, antivirus software, satellite phones, life-jacket beacons.

---

### 2.3 Framework C — Industrial / B2B Products (Material & Parts, Capital Items, Supplies & Services)

#### C.1 Materials & Parts
Raw materials (silicon wafers) and manufactured materials & parts (capacitors, ICs, PCBs, fasteners).

#### C.2 Capital Items
Long-term investments that aid the buyer's production: **installations** (CNC machines, SMT pick-and-place lines, server racks) and **accessory equipment** (hand tools, test instruments, office computers).

#### C.3 Supplies & Business Services
Operating supplies (lubricants, solder paste, cleaning fluids) and maintenance & repair services (AMC contracts, calibration services).

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Classification Decision Matrix

The "formula" in product classification is essentially a **decision matrix** mapping product attributes to design parameters:

| Product Class | Useful Life (yr) | MTBF Target (hrs) | Cost Sensitivity | Repair-Friendly? | Compliance Burden |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Nondurable (convenience) | $\lt 1$ | $\sim 5{,}000$ | **Extreme** | No | Minimal |
| Durable — Consumer (shopping) | $2\text{–}5$ | $\sim 20{,}000$ | High | Sometimes | BIS, RoHS |
| Durable — Consumer (specialty) | $5\text{–}10$ | $\sim 40{,}000$ | Medium | Yes | BIS, CE, FCC |
| Industrial Capital | $10\text{–}20$ | $\gt 50{,}000$ | Medium | Yes (mandatory) | ISO 9001, IEC |
| Medical / Aerospace | $\gt 15$ | $\gt 100{,}000$ | Low | Yes (mandatory) | FDA, ISO 13485, AS9100 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** This matrix is used by *every Tier-1 OEM* (Samsung, Bosch, Siemens) during the **Concept-Stage Gate Review** of their Stage-Gate® NPD process. Mis-mapping a product to the wrong row results in a *design freeze failure* — a multi-crore rupee loss.

---

### 2.5 Classification by Life-Cycle Stage (PLC)

Every electronic product passes through four stages, each with distinct design/marketing tactics:

- **Introduction** — high R&D cost, low volume, design bugs surface, OTA patches issued.
- **Growth** — design-for-manufacturability (DFM) refinements, BOM cost reduction.
- **Maturity** — feature saturation, cosmetic refresh, design-for-sustainability (recycling).
- **Decline** — design-for-disassembly, end-of-life (EOL) announcements, last-time-buy notices.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Product Classification is primarily a *qualitative management concept*, not a numerical physics derivation. However, KTU 2024 Scheme examiners award **partial marks** for *structured analytical reasoning* and *justified classification*. The following analytical walkthrough demonstrates the **rigorous classification logic** an examiner expects in a 14-mark answer.

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: The "Classification Tree"

The classification of an electronic product can be formalized as a **top-down decision tree** with a deterministic path from the root to a unique leaf node. We model it as follows:

Let $P$ be the set of all products. We define the classification function:

$$
C : P \rightarrow \mathcal{C}
$$

where $\mathcal{C}$ is the codomain of classes. The function $C$ is a *composition of three binary sub-decisions*:

$$
C(p) = C_3\bigl(C_2(C_1(p))\bigr)
$$

**Step 1 — Apply $C_1$ (Tangibility test):**

$$
C_1(p) = \begin{cases} \text{Tangible}, & \text{if } \mathrm{Touch}(p) = \text{true} \\ \text{Intangible}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* A smartphone, a chip, a wire — all `Touch(p) = true`. A software license, a cloud service, an OTA update — all `Touch(p) = false`.

**Step 2 — If Tangible, apply $C_2$ (Durability test):**

$$
C_2(p \vert \text{Tangible}) = \begin{cases} \text{Durable}, & \text{if } L(p) \geq 1 \text{ year} \\ \text{Nondurable}, & \text{if } L(p) \lt 1 \text{ year} \end{cases}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* $L(p)$ is the *expected useful life*. A lithium AA battery has $L \approx 5 \text{ years} \Rightarrow$ Durable. A single-use vape has $L \lt 1 \text{ day} \Rightarrow$ Nondurable.

**Step 3 — Apply $C_3$ (Buyer-behaviour test):**

$$
C_3(p) = \begin{cases} \text{Convenience}, & \text{if } I(p) = \text{low} \land F(p) = \text{high} \\ \text{Shopping}, & \text{if } I(p) = \text{high} \land C(p) = \text{moderate} \\ \text{Specialty}, & \text{if } B(p) = \text{strong loyalty} \\ \text{Unsought}, & \text{if } A(p) = \text{low awareness} \end{cases}
$$

where $I(p)$ is the *involvement level*, $F(p)$ is the *purchase frequency*, $C(p)$ is the *comparison effort*, $B(p)$ is *brand loyalty*, and $A(p)$ is *awareness level*.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Classifying a *Wireless Bluetooth Earphone (₹2,500 segment)*

Let us rigorously classify the product $p = \text{"Boat Airdopes 141"}$:

**Step 1 — Tangibility Test**

$$
\mathrm{Touch}(\text{Boat Airdopes 141}) = \text{true}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow C_1(p) = \text{Tangible}
$$

**Step 2 — Durability Test**

$$
L(p) \approx 2 \text{ to } 3 \text{ years (with typical use)}
$$

Since $2 \text{ yr} \geq 1 \text{ yr}$:

$$
\Rightarrow C_2(p \vert \text{Tangible}) = \text{Durable}
$$

**Step 3 — Buyer-Behaviour Test**

- Involvement $I(p)$: *moderate* (some comparison, but not weeks of research)
- Purchase frequency $F(p)$: *moderate* (replacement every 2–3 years)
- Comparison effort $C(p)$: *moderate*
- Brand loyalty $B(p)$: *moderate to high* (Boat, boAt, Noise brands)
- Awareness $A(p)$: *high* (mass-marketed)

Therefore, the product lands in the **Shopping** quadrant:

$$
C_3(p) = \text{Shopping Product}
$$

**Final Classification (composite):**

$$
C(p) = C_3(C_2(C_1(p))) = \text{Durable Shopping Consumer Good}
$$

> **Engineering takeaway:** This classification tells the design team → use a *moderate-cost plastic enclosure*, *IPX4 sweat resistance*, *one-year warranty*, *BIS + RoHS compliance*, *BOM target ≤ ₹350*.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Design Implications of Each Class

For every class, the design team must lock-in the following *design parameters*:

| Class | Material Choice | Reliability Goal | Test Plan Depth | Marketing Spend |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Nondurable Convenience | Recycled ABS, thin copper | MTBF $\sim 5\text{k}$ hr | Basic safety only | High (visibility) |
| Durable Shopping | PC+ABS, aluminium alloys | MTBF $\sim 20\text{k}$ hr | Safety + EMC + drop test | Medium |
| Durable Specialty | Aerospace Al, Gorilla Glass | MTBF $\sim 40\text{k}$ hr | Full qualification suite | Brand-led |
| Industrial Capital | Steel, industrial plastics | MTBF $\gt 50\text{k}$ hr | IEC + ISO 9001 audits | Relationship-driven |
| Medical / Aerospace | Titanium, ceramics, hermetic | MTBF $\gt 100\text{k}$ hr | FDA / AS9100 audit | Regulatory-driven |
| Intangible Service | N/A (digital) | SLA 99.9% uptime | Penetration testing | Subscription-led |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When a 14-mark question says *"Classify the product and justify your design choices"*, the above matrix is the *exact analytical reasoning* expected. Marks are awarded for *linking classification → design parameter → engineering rationale*, NOT for merely naming the class.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Tree (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[All Electronic Products P]
    tangA{Tangible?}
    intA[Services and Intangible Goods]
    durA{Useful Life >= 1 yr?}
    nondurA[Nondurable Goods]
    durB[Durable Goods]
    buyerA{Buyer Behaviour?}
    convA[Convenience Products]
    shopA[Shopping Products]
    specA[Specialty Products]
    unsA[Unsought Products]
    indA[Industrial B2B Products]
    matA[Materials and Parts]
    capA[Capital Items]
    supA[Supplies and Services]
    plcA{PLC Stage?}
    introA[Introduction]
    growA[Growth]
    matB[Maturity]
    decA[Decline]

    rootA --> tangA
    tangA -- Yes --> durA
    tangA -- No --> intA
    durA -- No --> nondurA
    durA -- Yes --> durB
    durB --> buyerA
    buyerA -- Low Involvement --> convA
    buyerA -- High Involvement --> shopA
    buyerA -- Brand Loyal --> specA
    buyerA -- Low Awareness --> unsA
    rootA --> indA
    indA --> matA
    indA --> capA
    indA --> supA
    durB --> plcA
    plcA --> introA
    plcA --> growA
    plcA --> matB
    plcA --> decA
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow — Design Parameter Lock-In

```mermaid
graph LR
    clsA[Product Classification Output]
    paramA[Material Selection Module]
    paramB[Reliability Target Module]
    paramC[Compliance Module]
    paramD[Cost Target Module]
    paramE[Test Plan Module]
    outA[Design Specification Frozen]
    gateA[Stage Gate Review Pass]

    clsA --> paramA
    clsA --> paramB
    clsA --> paramC
    clsA --> paramD
    clsA --> paramE
    paramA --> outA
    paramB --> outA
    paramC --> outA
    paramD --> outA
    paramE --> outA
    outA --> gateA
```

### 4.3 Decision-Mapping Flow — From Classification to Compliance

```mermaid
graph TD
    inputA[New Product Idea]
    fnA[Step 1: Classify by Tangibility]
    fnB[Step 2: Classify by Durability]
    fnC[Step 3: Classify by Buyer Behaviour]
    fnD[Step 4: Classify by Life Cycle Stage]
    fnE[Step 5: Lock Design Parameters]
    opA[Design Specification Document]
    opB[BOM and Compliance Plan]

    inputA --> fnA --> fnB --> fnC --> fnD --> fnE --> opA
    fnE --> opB
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**  
*Define the term "product" as used in electronic product design. Differentiate between a **durable good** and a **nondurable good** with two electronic examples each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
A **product** is anything offered to a market that can satisfy a need or want, including physical goods, services, experiences, or ideas (Kotler).  
A **durable good** has a useful life of **≥ 1 year** and is designed for repeated use. *Electronic examples:* Smartphones, refrigerators, laptops.  
A **nondurable good** is consumed in one or few uses, with useful life **< 1 year**. *Electronic examples:* Disposable AA batteries, single-use earphones, USB promotional cables.  
*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for durable + examples, 1 mark for nondurable + examples.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**  
*List the four categories of consumer products based on **buyer behaviour**. Give one electronic example of an **unsought product** and explain why it is classified as unsought.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
The four categories are: (1) **Convenience**, (2) **Shopping**, (3) **Specialty**, (4) **Unsought**.  
An **unsought product** is one the consumer either does not know about or knows about but does not actively want. *Electronic example:* a **fire alarm** or **extended warranty for a smartphone** — buyers do not think of purchasing it under normal conditions, and aggressive marketing is required.  
*(1 mark for listing, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for justification.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]*

**(a)** Classify the product **"Apple iPhone 15 Pro Max"** using the three frameworks studied in Module 1. Justify each classification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Based on your classification, derive the **design parameters** (material, reliability target, compliance, cost-sensitivity, and test plan) that the Apple design team would lock in during the **Concept-Stage Gate Review**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

| Framework | Classification | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Durability & Tangibility | **Durable Good** (Tangible) | Useful life of iPhone ≈ 5+ years with updates; physical device. |
| Consumer Behaviour | **Specialty Product** | Extreme brand loyalty, premium positioning, no substitutes accepted by loyal buyers. |
| Life-Cycle Stage | **Maturity** (15th generation; incremental refresh) | Saturated feature set, yearly cosmetic refresh, EOL planning underway. |

*(Award 2 marks per framework + 1 mark for overall coherent conclusion.)*

**(b) [7 marks]**

| Design Parameter | Locked-in Value | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Material | **Titanium frame, Ceramic Shield glass** | Premium feel, light weight, scratch-resistance → aligns with specialty positioning. |
| Reliability (MTBF) | $\sim 40{,}000$ hours target | Premium consumer device; long software support window. |
| Compliance | **BIS (India), CE (EU), FCC (US), RoHS, REACH** | Multi-region launch; specialty buyers expect global regulatory safety. |
| Cost Sensitivity | **Low** (BoM cost not a constraint) | Specialty premium pricing (> ₹1.5 lakh); focus on experience, not cost. |
| Test Plan | **Full qualification suite** — drop, water (IP68), thermal, EMC, 5-year accelerated aging | Justified by specialty class & 5+ year useful life. |

*(Award 1 mark per parameter + 2 marks for engineering rationale linking back to classification.)*

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted — Internal Choice Alternative]*

**(a)** Explain the **classification of industrial (B2B) products** with suitable electronic-industry examples. Why is the design approach for industrial products fundamentally different from consumer products? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A startup is designing a **low-cost IoT-based soil-moisture sensor** for Indian farmers (target price ₹500). Classify this product using all three frameworks and discuss the **design trade-offs** the engineering team must navigate. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**  
Industrial (B2B) products are classified into three groups:

1. **Materials & Parts** — raw materials (silicon wafers, copper foil) and manufactured parts (capacitors, ICs, connectors). These enter the buyer's product directly.
2. **Capital Items** — installations (SMT pick-and-place line, server racks) and accessory equipment (oscilloscopes, soldering stations). They aid production but are not part of the finished product.
3. **Supplies & Services** — operating supplies (solder paste, flux, nitrogen gas) and maintenance services (AMC contracts, ISO calibration).

**Fundamental difference from consumer products:**

| Dimension | Consumer (B2C) | Industrial (B2B) |
|---|---|---|
| Buyer | Individual, emotional | Engineer/Procurement, rational |
| Volume | High, distributed | Low, concentrated |
| Customization | Mass-market, uniform | Tailor-made, specs-driven |
| Relationship | Transactional | Long-term partnership |
| Service | Minimal | **Critical** (SLAs, AMCs) |

*(1 mark per category + 2 marks for difference table + 1 mark for conclusion.)*

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Classification:**

| Framework | Class | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Durability | **Durable** (sensor life ≈ 2–3 years in field) | Outdoor installation, weather exposure, multiple crop cycles. |
| Buyer Behaviour | **Shopping Product** (if farmer buys directly) or **Industrial / B2B** (if FPO/NGO procures) | Comparison-shopping, price-sensitive, feature-driven. |
| Life-Cycle Stage | **Introduction** | New product category, low awareness, pilot deployments. |

**Design Trade-offs (4 critical ones — 1.5 marks each):**

1. **Cost vs. Reliability** — ₹500 retail forces sub-₹150 BoM; cannot use industrial-grade ICs. Use **automotive-grade (-40°C to +85°C)** as compromise.
2. **Battery Life vs. Connectivity** — LoRa (long-range, low-power) preferred over Wi-Fi or 4G for rural India.
3. **Ingress Protection vs. Cost** — IP65 minimum for monsoon exposure, but avoid IP67+ cost penalty.
4. **Repair vs. Replace** — Sealed enclosure (replace) chosen over modular repair, since replacement cost < farmer's cost of repair-shop visit.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**  
> 1. ❌ **Naming a class without justification** → Zero marks for mere labelling. Always pair *class* with *why*.  
> 2. ❌ **Confusing "convenience" with "cheap"** — Convenience is about *low buyer involvement*, not low price. A ₹1 lakh Mac Pro for a designer who buys it routinely is still a **convenience** product.  
> 3. ❌ **Forgetting the three-framework multi-axis view** — KTU 2024 examiners expect you to apply **all three frameworks** (durability, buyer behaviour, life-cycle) for a complete classification.  
> 4. ❌ **Skipping the link to engineering design parameters** — A classification without downstream design implication is incomplete.  
> 5. ❌ **Calling services "products" without disclaimers** — Always specify *intangible* and use the right examples (cloud, SaaS, OTA).

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Product = Anything** that satisfies a need/want — tangible, intangible, service, idea, person, place, organization, experience.
- **Durability cut-off:** useful life **≥ 1 year** = Durable, else Nondurable.
- **Four consumer classes** (by buyer behaviour): **Convenience, Shopping, Specialty, Unsought**.
- **Three industrial classes:** Materials & Parts, Capital Items, Supplies & Services.
- **Services** are intangible, inseparable, variable, perishable.
- **PLC stages:** Introduction → Growth → Maturity → Decline.
- **Classification → Design Parameters** is a *one-way deterministic link*; the matrix in Section 3.3 is your KTU cheat sheet.
- **MTBF benchmark:** consumer ~20k hr, industrial ~50k hr, medical/aerospace >100k hr.
- **Always apply all three frameworks** in a 14-mark answer for full marks.
- **Justify, then conclude** — classification + rationale + engineering implication.
- **Kotler & Assael** are the authority references for these frameworks (do not invent others).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: New Product Development Process. Product Design Methodology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT862 - ELECTRONIC PRODUCT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** New Product Development Process. Product Design Methodology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 New Product Development (NPD) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **New Product Development (NPD)** is the complete, cross-functional journey an organization undertakes to transform a market opportunity or technological breakthrough into a sellable, manufactured, and supported **electronic product**. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (PEERT862), NPD is treated as a structured, gated workflow that fuses **engineering design**, **marketing analysis**, and **manufacturing strategy** into a single coherent pipeline.

In the context of electronic product design, NPD is *not* simply "inventing a gadget." It is the disciplined sequence of **idea → concept → prototype → production → launch → lifecycle support**, governed by customer requirements, regulatory standards (e.g., **BIS**, **CE**, **FCC**, **RoHS**), and economic feasibility.

### 1.2 Product Design Methodology — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Product Design Methodology** is the systematic, iterative set of engineering procedures used to convert a *product concept* (a "what") into a *product specification* and finally into a *detailed design* (a "how"). For electronic products, it spans **system-level architecture**, **circuit design**, **PCB layout**, **firmware integration**, **industrial (mechanical) design**, and **Design for Manufacturability (DFM)**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: Building a House**
> Think of NPD like building a custom home:
> - **Idea Generation** = "I wish I had a bigger kitchen."
> - **Screening** = "Can we afford a bigger kitchen?"
> - **Concept Development** = An architect sketches a layout.
> - **Business Analysis** = Bank approves the loan (budget check).
> - **Product Development** = Workers pour concrete, lay bricks.
> - **Test Marketing** = Friends and family tour the home and give feedback.
> - **Commercialization** = You move in and host a housewarming (public launch).
>
> **Product Design Methodology** is the *architect's blueprint* used during the construction phase — it specifies *every wall, wire, and window* so the house stands strong, is safe, and is aesthetically pleasing.

### 1.4 Why This Topic Is Critical for KTU 2024 Electronics Engineers

| Aspect | Relevance to an Electronics Engineer |
|---|---|
| **Idea → Product Gap** | Most engineering graduates have technical skills but lack the *process* to commercialize them. |
| **Industry Expectation** | Companies hiring from Kerala (Tata Elxsi, VVDN, SFO Technologies, etc.) require NPD awareness. |
| **Regulatory Compliance** | Electronics is heavily regulated; NPD embeds compliance from day one. |
| **Cost & Time-to-Market** | Poor methodology = cost overruns and missed launches. |
| **Sustainability (NEP 2020)** | Modern NPD must address **e-waste**, **energy efficiency**, and **recyclability**. |

### 1.5 The 7 Generic Stages of NPD (Industry Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this 7-stage sequence — it appears almost verbatim in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions.
> 1. **Idea Generation** (brainstorming, customer inputs, R&D push)
> 2. **Idea Screening** (drop bad ideas early — R-W-W screen: *Real, Win, Worth it*)
> 3. **Concept Development & Testing** (concept is tested on target users)
> 4. **Business Analysis** (sales forecast, cost analysis, profitability)
> 5. **Product Development** (engineering design, prototypes, tooling)
> 6. **Test Marketing** (limited release, focus groups, beta units)
> 7. **Commercialization** (full-scale launch, distribution, support)

These 7 stages are sometimes called the **"Booz, Allen & Hamilton 7-Stage NPD Model"** (a classic framework widely cited in engineering management).

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Foundational Pillars of Product Design Methodology

Modern electronic product design is built on three pillars, each addressing a different "view" of the product:

**Pillar A — Market-Driven Design (The "Why")**
- Identifies *user needs*, *willingness to pay*, and *competitive landscape*.
- Tools: **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)**, **House of Quality (HoQ)**, market surveys, **Kano Model**.
- Output: A **Marketing Requirements Document (MRD)**.

**Pillar B — Engineering Design (The "How")**
- Converts MRD into **Design Specifications (DS)**.
- Architecture selection (single-MCU vs. SoC vs. distributed), circuit topology, BOM, firmware architecture.
- Output: **Engineering drawings, schematics, BOM, Gerber files, firmware source code**.

**Pillar C — Manufacturing & Lifecycle Design (The "Make & Sustain")**
- **Design for Manufacturability (DFM)**, **Design for Test (DFT)**, **Design for Assembly (DFA)**, **Design for Environment (DFE)**.
- Output: **Pilot production line, test fixtures, service manuals, end-of-life plan**.

### 2.2 Ulrich, Eppinger & Yang's Product Development Process (Industry Reference)

The widely-accepted **6-phase product development process** (as taught in top engineering schools and used by KTU examiners) breaks down as:

1. **Phase 0 — Planning:** Mission statement, identification of customer needs, market segmentation, technology assessment.
2. **Phase 1 — Concept Development:** Concept generation (internal/external), concept selection (scoring matrix), final concept, project planning.
3. **Phase 2 — System-Level Design:** Functional decomposition, design structure matrix, interface definition, **Functional Block Diagram (FBD)**.
4. **Phase 3 — Detail Design:** Component selection, materials, tolerances, **Design for X (DFX)**, prototyping, alpha testing.
5. **Phase 4 — Testing & Refinement:** Beta testing, performance verification, reliability (HALT/HASS), regulatory certification.
6. **Phase 5 — Production Ramp-Up:** Pilot run, tooling, supply chain, training, full launch.

### 2.3 Stage-Gate® Process (Cooper's Model)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often ask: *"Explain the Stage-Gate process."* The **Stage-Gate® model** by Dr. Robert G. Cooper is the *de facto* industry standard — used by 75%+ of Fortune 500 firms.

Each **Stage** is a set of activities; each **Gate** is a quality checkpoint where go/kill/recycle decisions are made.

**Typical 5-Stage, 5-Gate Process:**

| Gate | Stage | Decision Criteria |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| Gate 1 | Idea Screening | Strategic fit, technical feasibility |
| Gate 2 | Scoping | Market size, competitive advantage |
| Gate 3 | Business Case | ROI, NPV, payback period |
| Gate 4 | Development | Prototype performance, design freeze |
| Gate 5 | Launch | Production readiness, supply chain |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Part A (3 marks), the following numerical formulas are commonly asked. Memorize them.**

| # | Formula / Metric | Equation | Where Used |
|:--:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Total Investment}} \times 100\%$ | Business Analysis stage |
| 2 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{\text{CF}_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}}$ | Capital budgeting, gate decision |
| 3 | **Payback Period** | $\text{Payback} = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$ | Quick feasibility check |
| 4 | **Break-Even Point (units)** | $\text{BEP} = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{Price} - \text{Variable Cost}}$ | Commercialization decision |
| 5 | **Development Cost Estimate (COCOMO-like)** | $C = a \cdot K^{b}$ where $K$ = product complexity (KLOC or function points) | Rough cost projection |
| 6 | **Time-to-Market (TTM) Value** | $\text{Profit}_{\text{lead}} = P \cdot \Delta t \cdot (1 - e^{-\lambda \Delta t})$ | Justifying fast NPD |
| 7 | **Concept Screening Score** | $S_{i} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_{j} \cdot r_{ij}$ (weighted scoring) | Concept selection |
| 8 | **Design Structure Matrix (DSM) Coupling)** | $\text{Sequencing Cost} = \sum_{i \neq j} \vert c_{ij} \vert \cdot \vert \pi_{i} - \pi_{j} \vert$ | Project planning |
| 9 | **Reliability (MTBF)** | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = \dfrac{T}{N_{f}}$ | Test & Refinement stage |
| 10 | **DFM Index** | $\text{DFM}_{I} = \dfrac{N_{\text{ideal}}}{N_{\text{actual}}}$ | DFM analysis |

**Boundary / Limitation Notes:**
- **ROI** ignores time value of money — use **NPV** for long projects.
- **COCOMO** is calibrated for software; for hardware use **function-point** or **analogy-based** estimation.
- **BEP** assumes linear cost & revenue (rare in electronics with learning curves).
- **MTBF** assumes constant failure rate (only valid in *useful life* region of the bathtub curve).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Insight:** The same **Stage-Gate** process is used by Apple (iPhone), Tesla (autopilot updates), and Intel (chip launches). For an electronics engineer in Kerala, understanding the **Gate 3 business case** is what gets your prototype funded; mastering **Gate 4 development** is what gets it manufactured.

### 2.6 Design Thinking vs. Traditional Methodology

| Aspect | Traditional (Waterfall) | Design Thinking (Iterative) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Starting point | Spec sheet | User empathy |
| Direction | Linear: Idea → Launch | Cyclic: Empathize → Define → Ideate → Prototype → Test |
| Failure handling | Late discovery (costly) | Early discovery (cheap) |
| Best for | Mature, well-understood products | Novel, user-centric electronics |
| KTU exam angle | Stage-Gate, Booz-Allan | Design Thinking (5 stages) |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivative Work #1: Concept Screening via Weighted Scoring Model

When a product design team generates $m$ candidate concepts, the $i$-th concept is scored against $n$ criteria. The model is derived as follows.

**Step 1 — Establish selection criteria.**
For an electronic product (e.g., a smart wearable), the criteria $C_{j}$ may be: power efficiency, cost, ergonomics, RF performance, manufacturability, novelty.

**Step 2 — Assign weights.**
The product manager assigns weights $w_{j}$ such that:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_{j} = 1$$

**Step 3 — Score each concept.**
A panel of experts rates concept $i$ on criterion $j$ with a score $r_{ij} \in [0, 10]$.

**Step 4 — Compute weighted score.**

$$S_{i} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_{j} \cdot r_{ij}$$

**Step 5 — Rank concepts.**
Sort $S_{i}$ in descending order; the concept with $S_{\max}$ is selected.

**Worked Example (Electronic Smart Sensor):**
Three concepts (A, B, C) evaluated on four criteria:

| Criterion | Weight $w_{j}$ | A | B | C |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Cost | 0.30 | 7 | 8 | 5 |
| Power | 0.25 | 6 | 7 | 9 |
| Size | 0.20 | 8 | 6 | 7 |
| Reliability | 0.25 | 9 | 8 | 8 |

**Step 6 — Compute scores.**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{A} &= (0.30)(7) + (0.25)(6) + (0.20)(8) + (0.25)(9) \\
S_{A} &= 2.10 + 1.50 + 1.60 + 2.25 = 7.45
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{B} &= (0.30)(8) + (0.25)(7) + (0.20)(6) + (0.25)(8) \\
S_{B} &= 2.40 + 1.75 + 1.20 + 2.00 = 7.35
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{C} &= (0.30)(5) + (0.25)(9) + (0.20)(7) + (0.25)(8) \\
S_{C} &= 1.50 + 2.25 + 1.40 + 2.00 = 7.15
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Final ranking:** $S_{A} = 7.45 > S_{B} = 7.35 > S_{C} = 7.15$

> **Decision: Concept A is selected for development.**

### 3.2 Derivative Work #2: Break-Even Analysis for an Electronic Product

A startup is launching a **smart IoT plug** at a target price of ₹1,200. Fixed costs (tooling, certification, marketing) total **₹50,00,000**. Variable cost per unit is **₹600**.

**Step 1 — Write the BEP equation.**

$$\text{BEP} = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{Price} - \text{Variable Cost}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{BEP} &= \dfrac{50,00,000}{1200 - 600} \\
\text{BEP} &= \dfrac{50,00,000}{600} \\
\text{BEP} &= 8,333.33 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to revenue.**

$$\text{BEP Revenue} = 8,334 \times 1200 = \text{₹ } 1,00,00,080$$

**Step 4 — Engineering insight.** The startup must sell at least **8,334 units** to break even. If the market forecast is 15,000 units, the project is viable. If only 5,000 units are expected, the design team must either **reduce BoM cost** or **increase price** (or **abandon the project**).

### 3.3 Derivative Work #3: NPV Calculation for NPD Investment

A company must invest **₹1 crore** in Year 0 to develop a product. Expected cash inflows for 4 years: **₹30L, ₹40L, ₹50L, ₹60L**. Discount rate $r = 12\%$.

**Step 1 — Apply NPV formula.**

$$\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{\text{CF}_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute each discounted cash flow.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{DF}_{0} &= \dfrac{-1,00,00,000}{(1.12)^{0}} = -1,00,00,000 \\
\text{DF}_{1} &= \dfrac{30,00,000}{(1.12)^{1}} = \dfrac{30,00,000}{1.12} = 26,78,571 \\
\text{DF}_{2} &= \dfrac{40,00,000}{(1.12)^{2}} = \dfrac{40,00,000}{1.2544} = 31,88,775 \\
\text{DF}_{3} &= \dfrac{50,00,000}{(1.12)^{3}} = \dfrac{50,00,000}{1.4049} = 35,59,041 \\
\text{DF}_{4} &= \dfrac{60,00,000}{(1.12)^{4}} = \dfrac{60,00,000}{1.5735} = 38,13,025
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Sum the discounted flows.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV} &= -1,00,00,000 + 26,78,571 + 31,88,775 + 35,59,041 + 38,13,025 \\
\text{NPV} &= +32,39,412
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Decision.** Since $\text{NPV} > 0$, the project is **financially viable** and should proceed past Gate 3.

| NPD Stage (Booz-Allan) | Design Methodology Activity | Key Deliverable | KTU Marks Weight |
|:---|:---|:---|:---:|
| Idea Generation | Brainstorming, market research | Idea bank (50+ concepts) | 2 |
| Idea Screening | R-W-W filter, SWOT | Shortlist (5–10) | 2 |
| Concept Development | Sketches, block diagrams, FBD | Concept document | 3 |

| Commercialization | Production, distribution, support | Product in market | 2 |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Q1.** List and briefly explain the stages of the **New Product Development (NPD) process**.

**Model Answer (10 lines for 3 marks):**
The 7 stages of NPD are:
1. **Idea Generation** — Brainstorming, customer inputs, and R&D exploration to create a pool of product ideas.
2. **Idea Screening** — Evaluating ideas using the *R-W-W* filter (Real, Win, Worth pursuing) to drop infeasible options early.
3. **Concept Development & Testing** — Converting surviving ideas into detailed concepts and testing them on target users.
4. **Business Analysis** — Estimating sales, costs, ROI, NPV, and break-even point to confirm commercial viability.
5. **Product Development** — Engineering design, prototyping, firmware development, and tooling.
6. **Test Marketing** — Limited release (beta units, focus groups) to validate the product in real conditions.
7. **Commercialization** — Full-scale production, distribution, marketing, and after-sales support.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q2.** What is the **Stage-Gate® process**? Mention any 3 gates with their decision criteria.

**Model Answer:**
The **Stage-Gate® process** (developed by Dr. Robert G. Cooper) is a project management framework where a product innovation process is divided into **Stages** (sets of activities) separated by **Gates** (decision points). At each gate, managers review deliverables and choose Go, Kill, Hold, or Recycle.

| Gate | Decision Criteria |
|:---|:---|
| **Gate 1 — Idea Screening** | Strategic fit, technical feasibility, alignment with company vision |
| **Gate 3 — Business Case** | ROI > hurdle rate, NPV > 0, payback within target years |
| **Gate 5 — Launch** | Production readiness, supply chain confirmed, certifications obtained |

### 5.3 Detailed Model Solutions

#### **Solution to Question A (a) — 7 Marks**

**Stages of NPD** (refer to Section 4.1 for diagram):

1. **Idea Generation (1 mark):** Sources include internal R&D, customer feedback, competitor analysis, technology trends, and brainstorming sessions. *Modern electronics example:* ideas for a new AI-based wearable come from health-trend analysis.

2. **Idea Screening (1 mark):** Apply the **R-W-W** filter — *Real (can we build it?), Win (can we beat competition?), Worth (will it make money?)*. Reject weak ideas early to save cost.

3. **Concept Development & Testing (1 mark):** Develop the most promising idea into a *product concept* (sketch + positioning statement). Test via focus groups, surveys.

4. **Business Analysis (1 mark):** Estimate **market size, sales forecast, costs, ROI, NPV, payback period**. Critical financial gate.

5. **Product Development (1 mark):** Engineering design phase — schematics, PCB layout, firmware, mechanical design, prototyping, and alpha testing.

6. **Test Marketing (1 mark):** Limited release, beta units, soft launch, or pilot in a small geographic region.

7. **Commercialization (1 mark):** Full production, launch event, distribution setup, marketing campaign, after-sales service.

**Why Idea Screening is Most Critical (1 bonus mark for clarity):**
Idea screening happens at the very top of the *cost-of-change curve*. Once a poor idea moves into development, the company has already invested heavily (in design, tooling, certification). **Failing fast and cheap** at the screening stage preserves capital for viable projects.

---

#### **Solution to Question A (b) — 7 Marks**

**Stage-Gate® Process Diagram** (4 marks):

Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for the structure. Explain that it has 5 stages separated by 5 gates.

**5 Gates and Decision Criteria** (2 marks — table format expected):

| Gate | Decision Criteria |
|:---|:---|
| Gate 1 — Idea Screen | Strategic fit, feasibility, R-W-W |
| Gate 2 — Scoping | Market attractiveness, competitive advantage |
| Gate 3 — Business Case | ROI, NPV > 0, payback < target |
| Gate 4 — Development | Design freeze, prototype performance |
| Gate 5 — Launch | Production capacity, supply chain ready |

**Comparison with Waterfall Model** (1 mark):

| Aspect | Stage-Gate | Waterfall |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Decision points | Multiple gates | Mostly end-of-phase |
| Iteration | Encouraged (recycle) | Rare, costly |
| Risk | Managed incrementally | Discovered late |
| Best for | Innovative, risky products | Well-understood, stable products |

---

#### **Solution to Question B (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (1 mark):** Product design methodology is a *systematic procedure* to translate customer needs into a manufacturable product specification.

**Ulrich-Eppinger 6 Phases** (5 marks — explain each with a sentence, plus draw flowchart):

- **Phase 0 — Planning:** Identify customer needs; create a *mission statement*.
- **Phase 1 — Concept Development:** Generate, select, and test concepts.
- **Phase 2 — System-Level Design:** Define functional architecture, decomposing the product into subsystems; create a **Functional Block Diagram**.
- **Phase 3 — Detail Design:** Define part geometry, materials, tolerances; design schematics, PCB, firmware; apply **DFM/DFT/DFA**.
- **Phase 4 — Testing & Refinement:** Alpha and beta prototypes; performance, reliability, EMC/safety tests; iterate.
- **Phase 5 — Production Ramp-Up:** Pilot run, tooling, supply chain, training; full production.

**Flowchart (1 mark):** Draw the phase-flow diagram (refer Section 4.2).

---

#### **Solution to Question B (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- Fixed Cost (FC) = ₹20,00,000
- Variable Cost per unit (VC) = ₹800
- Selling Price (P) = ₹1,600
- Expected sales = 8,000 units

**Step 1 — Compute contribution per unit.** (1 mark)

$$\text{Contribution} = P - VC = 1600 - 800 = 800 \text{ per unit}$$

**Step 2 — Apply BEP formula.** (1 mark)

$$\text{BEP} = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{Price} - \text{Variable Cost}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.** (1 mark)

$$\text{BEP} = \dfrac{20,00,000}{1600 - 800} = \dfrac{20,00,000}{800} = 2,500 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4 — BEP revenue.** (1 mark)

$$\text{BEP Revenue} = 2500 \times 1600 = \text{₹ } 40,00,000$$

**Step 5 — Projected profit at 8,000 units.** (2 marks)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Revenue} &= 8000 \times 1600 = \text{₹ } 1,28,00,000 \\
\text{Total Variable Cost} &= 8000 \times 800 = \text{₹ } 64,00,000 \\
\text{Total Cost} &= \text{FC} + \text{VC} = 20,00,000 + 64,00,000 = \text{₹ } 84,00,000 \\
\text{Profit} &= 1,28,00,000 - 84,00,000 = \text{₹ } 44,00,000
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Since expected sales (8,000) >> BEP (2,500), the project is **highly viable**, with a projected profit of **₹44 lakhs** and a **profit margin of 34.4%**.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make in This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "Idea Generation" with "Concept Development."** Idea generation is *raw ideas*; concept development is *refined, tested product concepts*. Examiners deduct ½ mark for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting to label Gates in the diagram.** If you draw a Stage-Gate flow but don't write "Gate 1, Gate 2..." the examiner will deduct 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up BEP and Payback Period.** BEP is in *units* (or revenue); Payback is in *years* (time to recover investment).
> 4. **Writing only 5 stages in NPD.** The classic model is **7 stages** (Booz-Allen). If you write 5 (Stage-Gate), you are referring to a *different* model — clarify in the answer.
> 5. **Skipping the formula in BEP questions.** Even if you give the right number, the formula earns a separate mark.
> 6. **No units in numerical answers.** Always write "**2,500 units**" and "**₹ 40,00,000**" — naked numbers lose marks.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 (NPD & Design Methodology)**

- **7 Stages of NPD (Booz-Allen):** Idea Gen → Screening → Concept Dev → Business Analysis → Product Dev → Test Marketing → Commercialization.
- **Stage-Gate Process (Cooper):** 5 stages, 5 gates, with Go/Kill/Hold/Recycle decisions.
- **Idea Screening Filter:** R-W-W → Real, Win, Worth it.
- **6 Phases of Ulrich-Eppinger:** Planning → Concept Dev → System Design → Detail Design → Testing → Production Ramp-up.
- **Design Thinking (5 stages):** Empathize → Define → Ideate → Prototype → Test (iterative cycle).
- **BEP formula:** $\text{BEP} = \dfrac{\text{FC}}{P - VC}$ — memorize *symbol meanings*.
- **NPV formula:** $\text{NPV} = \sum \dfrac{\text{CF}_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}}$ — NPV > 0 means *accept the project*.
- **Concept Selection:** Weighted scoring $S_{i} = \sum w_{j} r_{ij}$ with $\sum w_{j} = 1$.
- **Reliability metric:** $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$, valid only in the *useful life* region.
- **Design for X (DFX) family:** DFM, DFT, DFA, DFE — each addresses a *different* lifecycle concern.
- **Deliverables per stage:** MRD (marketing), DS (engineering), FBD (architecture), BOM (manufacturing), Gerber (PCB).
- **Kerala Industry Context:** Tata Elxsi, VVDN, SFO Technologies, NeST, and Keltron all follow Stage-Gate variants.
- **NEP 2020 / 2024 Scheme Emphasis:** Sustainability (e-waste, RoHS), design for environment, and ethical AI integration are *new* in 2024 answers.
- **One-line exam tagline:** *"A great product is not invented; it is developed through a disciplined, gated, customer-centric process."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Product Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEERT862 - ELECTRONIC PRODUCT DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Product Planning # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Product Planning** is the strategic, systematic process of defining, evaluating, and selecting product development projects to be undertaken by an organization. In the context of **Electronic Product Design (EPD)**, it represents the critical pre-design phase where market needs, technological feasibility, economic viability, and regulatory compliance are aligned to chart the trajectory of a new electronic product from conception to commercialization.
