```mermaid
    D2[Dielectric FR4 Core 1.20 mm]:::die
    L3[Layer 3 Inner Power Plane VCC Pour]:::pwr
    D3[Dielectric FR4 0.21 mm]:::die
    L4[Layer 4 Bottom Signal]:::sig
    V1[Through Via Plated Barrel]:::via
    V2[Blind Via Top to L2]:::via
    V3[Buried Via L2 to L3]:::via
    L1 --- D1
    D1 --- L2
    L2 --- D2
    D2 --- L3
    L3 --- D3
    D3 --- L4
    V1 -.- L1
    V1 -.- L4
    V2 -.- L1
    V2 -.- L2
    V3 -.- L2
    V3 -.- L3
    classDef sig fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7,color:#000
    classDef die fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#6c757d,color:#000
    classDef gnd fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef pwr fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef via fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Physical Integration Decision Tree

A functional decision-flow showing how an engineer selects between integration technologies.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start New Circuit Design] --> S1{Operating Frequency}
    S1 -- DC to 50 MHz --> S2{Required Current}
    S1 -- 50 to 500 MHz --> S3[Use SMT with 50 ohm Impedance Control]
    S1 -- Above 500 MHz --> S4[Use Stripline or Microstrip with RF Substrate]
    S2 -- Greater than 3 A --> S5[Choose THT for Power Components]
    S2 -- Less than 3 A --> S6[Choose SMT for Signal Components]
    S5 --> S7[Drill and Plated Through Holes]
    S6 --> S8[Surface Pads and Solder Paste]
    S7 --> S9[PCB Assembly and Test]
    S8 --> S9
    S3 --> S9
    S4 --> S9
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Physical Integration of Circuits" and list its three primary objectives.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Physical integration of circuits is the process of assembling electronic components and interconnections onto a physical substrate (typically a PCB) to form a functional, manufacturable product. The three primary objectives are:
1. **(1 mark)** To realize a **reliable electrical interconnection** between components that preserves signal integrity.
2. **(1 mark)** To ensure **thermal management** so junction temperatures stay within safe limits.
3. **(1 mark)** To achieve a **manufacturable, mechanically robust design** that obeys DFM/DFA rules.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Differentiate between blind vias and buried vias in a multilayer PCB.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** A **blind via** connects an *outer* PCB layer to one or more *inner* layers and is visible from only one side of the board.
- **(1 mark)** A **buried via** connects two or more *inner* layers only and is **completely hidden** inside the PCB, invisible from either outer surface.
- **(1 mark)** Buried/blind vias enable higher routing density and shorter stubs in high-speed designs, but they increase fabrication cost and require sequential lamination.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each module question is 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) and (b) usually carrying 7 marks each. Two alternative choices (A and B) are provided below — you answer **one**.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **physical integration hierarchy** of an electronic product. List the major tiers and the function of each tier. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **characteristic impedance of a microstrip line** on a PCB. State the formula and explain the role of the $\dfrac{w}{h}$ ratio. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

The physical integration hierarchy is a top-down decomposition from system intent to atomic interconnection.

1. **(2 marks)** **Tier 1 – System Level:** The complete product (e.g., smartphone), integrating multiple boards, displays, batteries, and mechanical housing.
2. **(1 mark)** **Tier 2 – Board/Module Level:** PCBs, flex circuits, and rigid-flex assemblies; each board hosts one or more functional blocks.
3. **(1 mark)** **Tier 3 – Substrate Level:** The PCB itself — dielectric substrate (FR-4, polyimide, ceramic) with copper layers.
4. **(1 mark)** **Tier 4 – Interconnection Level:** Vias, traces, pads, planes, and solder joints.
5. **(1 mark)** **Tier 5 – Component/Package Level:** Active and passive components in their respective packages (DIP, SOIC, QFP, BGA, CSP, QFN).
6. **(1 mark)** **Function of each tier:** converts logical intent into progressively finer physical realization while managing electrical, thermal, and mechanical constraints.

#### Model Solution to (b):

The derivation follows the standard microstrip procedure:

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define geometry — width $w$, thickness $t$, dielectric height $h$, dielectric constant $\varepsilon_r$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Express per-unit-length capacitance $C_a$ and inductance $L_a$ using the microstrip model.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply $Z_0 = \sqrt{L_a/C_a}$.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Substitute and simplify to obtain Wheeler's formula:

$$Z_0 = \dfrac{87}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r + 1.41}} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{5.98\,h}{0.8\,w + t}\right)$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Explain the role of the $w/h$ ratio: a larger $w/h$ ratio (wider trace or thinner dielectric) **lowers** $Z_0$; a smaller $w/h$ ratio **raises** $Z_0$. For $Z_0 = 50\ \Omega$ on FR-4, $w/h \approx 1.8$–$2.0$.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Mention that this $Z_0$ must match the driver's output impedance to avoid reflections that corrupt high-speed signals.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Compare **Through-Hole Technology (THT)** and **Surface Mount Technology (SMT)** across the parameters: mechanical strength, component density, frequency suitability, and assembly cost. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PCB trace carries $2.5\ \text{A}$ on $1\ \text{oz}$ copper. Using the IPC-2152 simplified relation, compute the minimum trace width for a $20^\circ\text{C}$ temperature rise. Take $k = 0.024$, $t = 1.4\ \text{mil}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**(1 mark each parameter × 4 = 4 marks, plus 3 marks for the comparison table or matrix)**

| Parameter | THT | SMT |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mechanical strength | High | Lower |
| Component density | Low | Very high |
| Frequency suitability | Poor (parasitic inductance from leads) | Excellent (short leads) |
| Assembly cost | Higher (manual/drilled) | Lower (automated pick-and-place) |

**(3 marks)** Additional commentary: SMT dominates modern design because of automation, density, and RF performance. THT remains preferred for connectors, large electrolytic capacitors, and high-current/high-voltage components. Modern designs use a **hybrid** approach — SMT for most components, THT for mechanical/electrical interfaces.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the IPC-2152 relation:

$$I = k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute $\Delta T^{0.44}$:
$$20^{0.44} \approx 3.79$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Substitute the values:

$$2.5 = 0.024 \cdot 3.79 \cdot A^{0.725}$$

$$2.5 = 0.091 \cdot A^{0.725}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Solve for $A^{0.725}$:
$$A^{0.725} = \dfrac{2.5}{0.091} \approx 27.47$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Raise both sides to the power $1/0.725 \approx 1.379$:
$$A = (27.47)^{1.379}$$

$$A \approx 27.47^{1.379} \approx 96.5\ \text{mil}^2$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Compute width:
$$w = \dfrac{A}{t} = \dfrac{96.5}{1.4} \approx 68.9\ \text{mil} \approx 1.75\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** State the design recommendation: **use a trace width of $\geq 1.8\ \text{mm}$** to provide a small safety margin.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Students frequently write "$2.5 \cdot 0.024 \cdot 3.79$" without isolating $A^{0.725}$ correctly — losing 2 marks.
> 2. Many forget to convert $A$ from mil$^2$ to actual width $w$ using $t$ — losing 1 mark.
> 3. Some write the answer without a **unit** — KTU deducts 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 4. Writing "$k = 0.024$" without specifying the units and reference (IPC-2152 internal trace) loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

- ☐ **Definition:** Physical integration = schematic $\rightarrow$ manufacturable PCB + components + enclosure.
- ☐ **Five Tiers:** System $\rightarrow$ Board $\rightarrow$ Substrate $\rightarrow$ Interconnect $\rightarrow$ Package.
- ☐ **THT vs SMT:** THT is robust and high-power; SMT is dense, automated, and RF-friendly.
- ☐ **FR-4 $\varepsilon_r$:** $\approx 4.3$ — remember this for $Z_0$ derivations.
- ☐ **Microstrip $Z_0$ formula:** $Z_0 = \dfrac{87}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r + 1.41}} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{5.98\,h}{0.8\,w + t}\right)$.
- ☐ **$w/h$ ratio:** For $50\ \Omega$ on FR-4, $w/h \approx 2$. Larger $w/h$ $\Rightarrow$ lower $Z_0$.
- ☐ **Vias:** Through (whole board), Blind (outer-to-inner), Buried (inner-to-inner, hidden).
- ☐ **IPC-2152:** $I = k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$; $k \approx 0.024$ for $1\ \text{oz}$ internal copper.
- ☐ **Copper weight:** $1\ \text{oz} = 35\ \mu\text{m} = 1.4\ \text{mil}$ thickness.
- ☐ **Thermal rule:** $T_j = T_a + P \cdot \theta_{JA}$ — must keep $T_j < T_{j,\max}$ (typically $125^\circ\text{C}$).
- ☐ **Annular ring:** $\geq 4\ \text{mil}$ for manufacturability.
- ☐ **DFM/DFA rules:** Always mentioned in KTU 14-mark answers for full credit.
- ☐ **High-speed design:** Match $Z_0$ to driver impedance ($50\ \Omega$ typical) to avoid reflections.
- ☐ **Power integrity:** Use **power planes** (not just traces) for $V_{CC}$ and **GND** to minimize loop inductance.
- ☐ **Thermal management:** Use **thermal vias**, **copper pours**, and **heat sinks** for $>1\ \text{W}$ components.
- ☐ **Always include units** in numerical KTU answers — marks are deducted otherwise.
- ☐ **Use `\vert` or `\mid`** (not bare `|`) when writing absolute values in your exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Packages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT862 - ELECTRONIC PRODUCT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Packages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Electronic Product Design**, the term **"Packages"** (often called **Electronic Packages** or **Semiconductor Packages**) refers to the **physical enclosure, housing, and interconnect structure** that encapsulates a bare semiconductor die (chip) to form a functional, board-mountable electronic component. A package serves as the critical **intermediary interface** between the microscopic silicon die (operating at nanometer scale) and the macroscopic Printed Circuit Board (PCB) operating at the millimeter/centimeter scale.

According to **JEDEC (Joint Electron Device Engineering Council)** and **IPC (Association Connecting Electronics Industries)** standards, a package must satisfy four fundamental engineering functions:

1. **Mechanical Protection** – Shielding the brittle silicon die from physical stress, vibration, shock, and handling damage.
2. **Environmental Protection** – Preventing ingress of moisture (humidity), dust, chemicals, and ionic contaminants that cause corrosion.
3. **Thermal Management** – Providing a low-resistance path for heat to flow from the die junction to the ambient environment.
4. **Electrical Interconnection** – Routing hundreds or thousands of signals between the die pads and the PCB via leads, balls, or pillars.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** For the course *PEERT862 – Electronic Product Design*, "Packages" broadly covers **(a) IC/Component Packages** (DIP, SOIC, QFP, BGA, QFN, CSP) and **(b) the overall product enclosure/packaging strategy** from a Design-for-Manufacturability (DFM) and Design-for-Assembly (DFA) perspective.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – The "Apartment Building" Model:**  
> Imagine the **silicon die** as a **single delicate light bulb** (the brain) inside a glass factory. This bulb is too fragile, too small, and has too many fragile wires to be connected directly to your home's electrical grid.
> 
> - The **IC Package** is the **apartment building** that houses the bulb.  
> - The **lead frame / substrate** inside is the building's internal wiring (corridors).  
> - The **pins/leads/balls** coming out of the package are the **electrical outlets** on the building's wall.  
> - The **plastic/epoxy body** is the building's concrete shell protecting it from rain, dust, and people kicking it.  
> - The **heat sink / thermal pad** is the building's **air-conditioning system** dumping heat to the atmosphere.  
> - Finally, the **PCB** is the **city's power grid** that the building plugs into.

Without the package, the silicon die is essentially **unusable** in any real-world product. The package transforms a fragile piece of silicon (a few mm²) into a robust, handleable, solderable, and manufacturable electronic component.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Metric | Typical Value / Standard |
|---|---|
| **Die Size Range** | **1 mm × 1 mm to 25 mm × 25 mm** |
| **Package Pitch (modern)** | **0.4 mm, 0.5 mm, 0.65 mm, 0.8 mm, 1.0 mm, 1.27 mm** |
| **Junction-to-Ambient Thermal Resistance (θJA)** | **20 °C/W to 100 °C/W** (package dependent) |
| **Operating Temperature Range (Commercial)** | **0 °C to +70 °C** |
| **Operating Temperature Range (Industrial)** | **−40 °C to +85 °C** |
| **Operating Temperature Range (Military/Aerospace)** | **−55 °C to +125 °C** |
| **Standard Package Heights** | **1.0 mm, 1.2 mm, 1.5 mm, 1.8 mm, 2.0 mm, 2.5 mm, 3.0 mm** |
| **Lead Count Range** | **3 pins (transistors) to >2500 pins (high-end FPGAs/Processors)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Term – "Pitch":** The center-to-center distance between two adjacent leads/pins. Smaller pitch = higher pin density but more difficult and expensive to manufacture and assemble.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Electronic Packages

Electronic packages are broadly classified along **two primary axes**:

### Axis 1: By Mounting Technology
```
ELECTRONIC PACKAGES
        │
        ├── (A) THROUGH-HOLE PACKAGES (THT – Through-Hole Technology)
        │       ├── DIP  (Dual In-line Package)
        │       ├── SIP  (Single In-line Package)
        │       ├── ZIP  (Zigzag In-line Package)
        │       ├── PGA  (Pin Grid Array)
        │       └── TO   (Transistor Outline, e.g., TO-92, TO-220)
        │
        └── (B) SURFACE MOUNT PACKAGES (SMT – Surface Mount Technology)
                ├── SOIC / SOP   (Small Outline IC / Small Outline Package)
                ├── QFP          (Quad Flat Package)
                ├── LQFP         (Low-profile QFP)
                ├── QFN / DFN    (Quad Flat No-leads / Dual Flat No-leads)
                ├── BGA          (Ball Grid Array)
                ├── CSP          (Chip Scale Package)
                ├── WLP / Fo-WLP (Wafer Level Package / Fan-out WLP)
                └── PoP          (Package-on-Package)
```

### Axis 2: By Sealing/Encapsulation Material
| Type | Material | Cost | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Plastic Encapsulated** | Epoxy Molding Compound (EMC) | **Low** | Consumer electronics, mobile phones, IoT |
| **Ceramic** | Alumina (Al₂O₃), Aluminum Nitride (AlN) | **High** | Military, aerospace, high-temp |
| **Hermetic Metal/Glass** | Kovar, glass-to-metal seals | **Very High** | Space, undersea cables, defense |
| **Embedded** | Package embedded in PCB substrate | **Medium** | Smart cards, RFID, medical implants |

## 2.2 Deep Dive: Through-Hole vs Surface Mount Packages

### (A) Through-Hole Technology (THT) Packages
Through-hole packages have **leads that pass through drilled holes** in the PCB and are soldered on the opposite side.

#### **DIP – Dual In-line Package**
- **Structure:** Rectangular plastic/ceramic body with **two parallel rows of leads** protruding from the long sides, bent downward at 90°.
- **Standard Pitch:** **2.54 mm (0.1 inch)** — the universal "imperial" pitch.
- **Lead Count:** Typically 4 to 64 pins.
- **Why it exists / "The Why":** Pioneered in the 1960s, DIP was the **first standardized IC package**. Easy to hand-solder, easy to insert into IC sockets, robust to mechanical handling.
- **Where used:** Educational kits (Arduino's ATmega328 in PDIP-28), legacy industrial controllers, prototyping boards, op-amps, simple logic ICs (7400 series).
- **Limitation:** Occupies large PCB real estate, **cannot be placed on both sides of PCB easily**, limited to ~64 pins due to lead fragility and body length.

#### **PGA – Pin Grid Array**
- **Structure:** Square ceramic/plastic body with **a grid of pins on the bottom** (e.g., 10×10, 12×12, 21×21).
- **Pin Pitch:** **2.54 mm** typically.
- **Why used:** Hosts high pin-count processors (e.g., older Intel Pentium, AMD Athlon CPUs) where the **entire bottom surface is utilized for I/O**, enabling 200–1000+ pins in a compact area.
- **Limitation:** Requires **expensive ZIF (Zero Insertion Force) sockets**; pins are easily bent.

---

### (B) Surface Mount Technology (SMT) Packages
SMT packages are **soldered directly onto PCB pads** without through-holes. This enables **double-sided assembly**, **smaller size**, **lower parasitic inductance**, and **automated pick-and-place manufacturing**.

#### **SOIC / SOP – Small Outline (IC) Package**
- **Structure:** Compact version of DIP with **gull-wing leads** on two sides, lower height.
- **Pitch:** **1.27 mm (0.05")** standard, **0.65 mm** in SSOP (Shrink-SOP), **0.5 mm** in TSSOP.
- **Why it exists:** **The first SMT replacement for DIP** — saves ~30–50% board area versus DIP.
- **Where used:** Op-amps, logic ICs, EEPROMs, microcontrollers (e.g., ATmega328 in SOIC-8 form).

#### **QFP – Quad Flat Package**
- **Structure:** Square/rectangular flat body with **gull-wing leads on all FOUR sides** (hence "Quad").
- **Pitch:** **0.4 mm, 0.5 mm, 0.65 mm, 0.8 mm, 1.0 mm**.
- **Lead Count:** **32 to 304+ pins**.
- **Variants:** LQFP (Low-profile QFP, height ≤ 1.6 mm), TQFP (Thin QFP), PQFP (Plastic QFP).
- **Where used:** Microcontrollers (e.g., STM32 in LQFP-64), DSPs, ASICs, communication chips.
- **Limitation:** Fine-pitch QFPs (< 0.5 mm) require **precise reflow profiling and AOI (Automated Optical Inspection)**.

#### **QFN / DFN – Quad (Dual) Flat No-leads**
- **Structure:** Flat package with **no protruding leads** — instead, **flat contact pads** on the bottom and sides. The die-attach paddle (exposed pad) on the bottom doubles as a **thermal pad**.
- **Pitch:** **0.4 mm, 0.5 mm, 0.65 mm**.
- **Why it exists:** Combines **small footprint, low inductance, and excellent thermal performance** (the exposed pad is soldered directly to PCB copper for heat dissipation).
- **Challenge:** **Solder joint inspection is difficult** (pads are hidden underneath the package). Requires **X-ray inspection** for quality control.

#### **BGA – Ball Grid Array**
- **Structure:** Package substrate with **an array of solder balls** (typically SAC305 — Sn96.5/Ag3.0/Cu0.5) on the bottom surface. The balls collapse during reflow to form solder joints.
- **Ball Pitch:** **0.5 mm, 0.65 mm, 0.8 mm, 1.0 mm, 1.27 mm**.
- **Ball Count:** **49 to >2000 balls** (high-end FPGAs, GPUs, CPUs).
- **Why it exists:** **Highest I/O density per unit area** of any mainstream package. **Self-aligning during reflow** (solder balls' surface tension pulls the package into perfect alignment).
- **Substrate Types:** **PBGA** (Plastic BGA), **CBGA** (Ceramic BGA), **TBGA** (Tape BGA), **SBGA** (Super BGA with heat spreader).
- **Limitation:** **Impossible to visually inspect solder joints** — requires **X-ray or CT-scan**. **Not rework-friendly** at fine pitches.

#### **CSP – Chip Scale Package**
- **Definition (per IPC/JEDEC):** A package whose **total area is no more than 1.2× the area of the bare die** it contains.
- **Why it exists:** The **ultimate in miniaturization** — package footprint is essentially die-size. Used in smartphones, wearables, medical implants.
- **Examples:** WLCSP (Wafer Level Chip Scale Package), µBGA, Flip-chip BGA.
- **Subtype – WLCSP (Wafer Level Chip Scale Package):** The package is built **directly on the wafer** before dicing. No interposer or substrate — the **solder balls are placed directly on the die pads** (with redistribution layer, RDL).

#### **PoP – Package-on-Package**
- **Structure:** Two or more BGA packages **stacked vertically** (e.g., an Application Processor stacked on top of a Memory package). Solder balls of the bottom package connect to the top package's substrate.
- **Why it exists:** **Saves horizontal board space** in smartphones and tablets where a logic chip and memory must be co-located. Stacking height typically **1.4 mm to 1.8 mm**.

---

## 2.3 Package Selection – The "Engineering Decision Tree"

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** In Electronic Product Design, the choice of package is driven by a **weighted multi-criteria decision matrix**. The key parameters are:

| # | Decision Parameter | Description | Weighting Trend |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pin Count (I/O count)** | How many signals must exit the die? | High I/O → BGA, QFP |
| 2 | **Size / Footprint** | PCB area available | Small → QFN, CSP, WLCSP |
| 3 | **Height Profile** | Maximum vertical space | Thin → LQFP, TQFP, CSP |
| 4 | **Thermal Dissipation (P_diss)** | Watts to be removed | High P → QFN, BGA with heat spreader, exposed pad |
| 5 | **Electrical Performance** | Frequency, signal integrity, inductance | High freq → BGA (shortest stubs) |
| 6 | **Cost per Unit** | BOM cost constraint | Lowest → SOIC, TSSOP; Highest → ceramic, BGA |
| 7 | **Assembly & Rework** | Volume production vs prototype | Hand-solderable → SOIC, DIP; X-ray → BGA |
| 8 | **Reliability Environment** | Temperature, humidity, vibration, shock | Harsh → ceramic, hermetic, QFN (no leads to bend) |
| 9 | **Standardization & Second-Source** | Is it JEDEC standard? Can multiple vendors supply? | Prefer JEDEC-standard packages |
| 10 | **Testability & Probing** | Access to die nodes | Peripheral leads (QFP) better than BGA |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_J = T_A + P_D \cdot \theta_{JA}$ | Junction temperature from ambient & power | °C, W, °C/W |
| 2 | $\theta_{JA} = \theta_{JC} + \theta_{CS} + \theta_{SA}$ | Total thermal resistance decomposition | °C/W |
| 3 | $P_D = \dfrac{T_J(\max) - T_A}{\theta_{JA}}$ | Maximum allowable power dissipation | W |
| 4 | $L_{\text{pkg}} = n \cdot p$ | Approximate package side length (n pins on one side, pitch p) | mm |
| 5 | $A_{\text{pkg}} \approx L_{\text{pkg}}^2$ | Approximate package footprint area (QFP) | mm² |
| 6 | $f_{\text{res}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_{\text{lead}} \cdot C_{\text{pad}}}}$ | Lead inductance–pad capacitance resonance | Hz |
| 7 | $\%\, \text{coverage} = \dfrac{A_{\text{ball grid}}}{A_{\text{die}}} \times 100$ | Ball grid area utilization (BGA design) | % |
| 8 | $N_{\text{balls}} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{L_{\text{die}}}{p_{\text{ball}}} \right\rfloor^2$ | Max balls in a full-array BGA at pitch p_ball | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{Yield} = e^{-\lambda \cdot D_0 \cdot A_{\text{die}}}$ | (Ref) Yield vs. die area — package choice affects cost-yield | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{Cost}_{\text{PCB\,area}} = \dfrac{\text{Price}_{\text{component}}}{A_{\text{footprint}}}$ | "Cost density" of a package on PCB | ₹/mm² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal formula #1** is the single most important equation a KTU examiner will test in this module. The relationship $T_J = T_A + P_D \cdot \theta_{JA}$ is a **linear model** of the die's thermal behavior assuming a steady-state heat flow — it is the *engineering sanity-check* before selecting a heat sink or cooling strategy.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Preferred Package | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Smartphones (Apple, Samsung)** | **PoP with WLCSP logic + LPDDR memory** | Extreme miniaturization, short interconnects for GHz DDR |
| **Automotive ECUs** | **QFN, PowerPAD SOIC, ceramic BGA** | High reliability, thermal performance, vibration resistance |
| **Aerospace & Defense** | **Ceramic PGA, Hermetic BGA, CCGA** | Hermeticity, temperature extremes, radiation hardness |
| **Industrial IoT sensors** | **QFN, WLCSP** | Low cost, small size, low power, moderate I/O |
| **Power Electronics (Motor drivers, MOSFETs)** | **TO-220, TO-247, D2PAK (TO-263), DFN** | High current, screw-mountable heatsinks, exposed pad |
| **Wearable / Hearables (AirPods)** | **WLCSP, Fan-out WLP** | Smallest possible footprint, flexible form-factor |
| **Medical Implants (Pacemakers)** | **Custom hermetic titanium-can + feedthroughs** | Biocompatibility, 10+ year hermeticity guarantee |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked "which package for which application," use the following rule-of-thumb: **"High pin count + high speed → BGA. Low pin count + hand-soldering → SOIC/DIP. High power + thermal → QFN/D2PAK. Smallest possible → WLCSP/CSP."**

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Junction Temperature Rise (Steady-State Thermal Model)

This is the **foundational thermal equation** that every KTU student must master for the "Packages" topic. We will derive $T_J$ from first principles.

### Assumptions
- Steady-state (time-invariant) heat flow.
- One-dimensional heat conduction from die → package → ambient.
- Heat flow is purely conductive through package, then convective to ambient air.
- No heat loss through leads (idealized worst-case).

### Step 1: Define thermal resistance (analogous to Ohm's law)

Thermal resistance is **defined** as the temperature rise per unit power dissipated, in exact analogy to electrical resistance $R = V/I$.

$$
\theta = \frac{\Delta T}{P_D}
$$

where $\Delta T$ is the temperature difference (°C) and $P_D$ is the dissipated power (W). The unit is **°C per Watt (°C/W)**.

### Step 2: Apply to the die-to-ambient path

By definition, the junction-to-ambient thermal resistance $\theta_{JA}$ relates the **junction temperature** $T_J$ to the **ambient temperature** $T_A$ through the dissipated power $P_D$:

$$
\theta_{JA} = \frac{T_J - T_A}{P_D}
$$

> **Textual logic:** The junction is the hottest point inside the die. Ambient is the still air surrounding the PCB. The difference between them, divided by the power flowing, gives the package's "thermal resistance" — its ability to resist heat flow.

### Step 3: Rearrange to solve for $T_J$

Multiplying both sides of Step 2 by $P_D$:

$$
P_D \cdot \theta_{JA} = T_J - T_A
$$

Adding $T_A$ to both sides:

$$
\boxed{\,T_J = T_A + P_D \cdot \theta_{JA}\,}
$$

This is the **canonical junction-temperature equation** in electronics thermal engineering.

### Step 4: Decompose $\theta_{JA}$ into the series-resistive thermal chain

In reality, heat flows from **die → die-attach → lead-frame/substrate → package body → PCB → ambient** (or → heat sink → ambient). Each interface adds a thermal resistance in **series**:

$$
\theta_{JA} = \theta_{JC} + \theta_{CS} + \theta_{SA}
$$

where:
- $\theta_{JC}$ = junction-to-case thermal resistance (°C/W) — package intrinsic.
- $\theta_{CS}$ = case-to-sink thermal resistance (°C/W) — interface material (TIM, thermal pad, paste).
- $\theta_{SA}$ = sink-to-ambient thermal resistance (°C/W) — depends on heat sink, airflow.

> **Textual logic:** This is **electrically identical to resistors in series** ($R_{total} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3$). Just as voltage drops across series resistors, temperature drops across each thermal "resistor."

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A microcontroller in an LQFP-64 package has $\theta_{JA} = 45 \text{ °C/W}$ on a 2-layer PCB (JEDEC standard test board). The ambient temperature inside an enclosed industrial enclosure is $T_A = 60 \text{ °C}$, and the chip dissipates $P_D = 0.8 \text{ W}$. The maximum allowed junction temperature for this silicon process is $T_{J(\max)} = 125 \text{ °C}$. Verify the design is safe.

**Solution:**

Substitute into the equation:

$$
T_J = T_A + P_D \cdot \theta_{JA}
$$

$$
T_J = 60 + (0.8) \times (45)
$$

$$
T_J = 60 + 36
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T_J = 96 \text{ °C}\,}
$$

Since $96 \text{ °C} < 125 \text{ °C}$, the design has a **thermal margin** of $125 - 96 = 29 \text{ °C}$ — the design is safe. ✓

If we had used a smaller package (say a QFN with the same die but $\theta_{JA} = 30 \text{ °C/W}$):

$$
T_J = 60 + (0.8) \times (30) = 60 + 24 = 84 \text{ °C}
$$

Margin improves to **41 °C** — QFN is thermally superior (no leads to act as thermal bottlenecks; exposed pad goes straight to PCB copper).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Allowable Power Dissipation

Rearranging the canonical equation to find the **maximum power** a package can safely dissipate, given an ambient and a junction-temperature limit:

$$
P_{D(\max)} = \frac{T_{J(\max)} - T_A}{\theta_{JA}}
$$

> **Textual logic:** Once you know how hot the die is allowed to get (a silicon-physics limit, typically 125 °C or 150 °C) and how hot the surrounding air is, the package's thermal resistance tells you the **upper bound on power**.

### Worked Example 2

> **Problem:** A linear regulator in a D2PAK (TO-263) package has $T_{J(\max)} = 150 \text{ °C}$, $\theta_{JA} = 35 \text{ °C/W}$ on a 1 oz copper 1-inch² pad, and operates in an environment at $T_A = 85 \text{ °C}$. What is the maximum continuous power dissipation?

$$
P_{D(\max)} = \frac{T_{J(\max)} - T_A}{\theta_{JA}} = \frac{150 - 85}{35} = \frac{65}{35}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{D(\max)} \approx 1.857 \text{ W}\,}
$$

**Design implication:** Above ~1.86 W on a D2PAK on a small pad, the junction temperature will exceed the silicon's rated limit — and the chip will **thermally run away** and fail. Either reduce power, lower ambient, increase PCB copper area, or attach a heat sink.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Approximate Package Footprint from Pin Count and Pitch

For a **Quad Flat Package (QFP)** with $N$ total leads, $N/4$ leads per side, and lead pitch $p$ (in mm), the approximate package side length $L$ is:

$$
L \approx (N/4) \cdot p + p = p \cdot \left(\frac{N}{4} + 1\right)
$$

> **Textual logic:** Each side has $N/4$ leads. Add one extra pitch unit to account for the **first and last lead's** body margin. The body length is approximately the total pitch span.

### Worked Example 3

> **Problem:** A QFP-100 (100 leads) with pitch $p = 0.5 \text{ mm}$. Estimate the package body size.

$$
L = 0.5 \times \left(\frac{100}{4} + 1\right) = 0.5 \times (25 + 1) = 0.5 \times 26
$$

$$
\boxed{\,L = 13 \text{ mm}\,}
$$

The actual JEDEC standard body size for LQFP-100 with 0.5 mm pitch is **14 mm × 14 mm** — our approximation is within ~7% (good engineering estimate).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Package Comparison

| Property | DIP-28 | SOIC-28 | LQFP-64 | QFN-64 | BGA-256 | WLCSP-64 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pitch (mm)** | 2.54 | 1.27 | 0.5 | 0.5 | 0.8 (ball) | 0.4 (ball) |
| **Body Size (mm)** | 35.6 × 14.0 | 17.9 × 7.5 | 10.0 × 10.0 | 9.0 × 9.0 | 17.0 × 17.0 | 4.5 × 4.5 |
| **Footprint Area (mm²)** | **498.4** | **134.3** | **100.0** | **81.0** | **289.0** | **20.25** |
| **Height (mm)** | 5.0 | 2.65 | 1.6 | 0.9 | 1.4 | 0.6 |
| **θJA (°C/W, 2-layer)** | 60 | 50 | 45 | 30 | 25 | 20 |
| **Max Pin Count** | ~64 | ~32 | ~304 | ~132 | >2000 | ~200 |
| **Hand-Solderable?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ⚠ Difficult | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| **Cost Index (1=lowest)** | 1 | 2 | 3 | 3 | 5 | 4 |
| **Visual Inspection?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ⚠ Limited | ❌ X-ray only | ❌ X-ray |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation from the matrix:** Moving from DIP → BGA reduces footprint by **~94%** for the same silicon. This is the **driving force** behind Moore's Law at the packaging level.

---

## 3.5 Decision Algorithm: Selecting the Right Package

```
START: Designer has a new IC die to package
        │
        ▼
   Q1: Pin count needed?
        │
   ┌────┴─────┐
   │          │
 ≤ 16      > 16
   │          │
   ▼          ▼
 SOIC       Q2: Hand-soldering / prototype needed?
 or           │
 DIP       ┌──┴──┐
           │     │
          YES    NO
           │     │
           ▼     ▼
        SOIC/  Q3: High speed (>1 GHz) signals?
         QFP    │
             ┌──┴──┐
             │     │
            YES    NO
             │     │
             ▼     ▼
            BGA   Q4: Power > 1 W?
                    │
                ┌───┴───┐
                │       │
               YES      NO
                │       │
                ▼       ▼
               QFN    Q5: Smallest size
              D2PAK   critical?
                │       │
                │    ┌──┴──┐
                │    │     │
                │   YES    NO
                │    │     │
                │    ▼     ▼
                │  WLCSP  LQFP
                │   CSP
                ▼
            QFN
           (with
         exposed
            pad)
```

> [!TIP]
> **For the KTU exam:** Memorize the **three golden rules** of package selection:
> 1. **Pin count dictates pitch and family** (low → SOIC; medium → QFP; high → BGA).
> 2. **Power dictates thermal path** (low → SOIC; high → QFN/D2PAK with exposed pad).
> 3. **Manufacturing volume dictates cost-tolerance** (low-volume prototype → SOIC/DIP; high-volume consumer → BGA/CSP).

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Internal Construction of a QFP (Quad Flat Package)

The following Mermaid block renders a **block-level functional architecture** showing the layered construction of a typical QFP IC package — from the silicon die on the inside to the PCB solder joints on the outside.

```mermaid
graph TD
    siliconDie["Silicon Die\nactive circuit side up"]
    dieAttach["Die Attach Material\nEpoxy or Solder"]
    leadFrame["Lead Frame\ncopper alloy 194 or C19400"]
    paddle["Die Attach Paddle\nDAP exposed copper"]
    bondWires["Gold Bond Wires\n25 micron diameter"]
    innerLeads["Inner Lead Tips\nbonding targets"]
    outerLeads["Outer Gull-Wing Leads\nsolderable surface finish"]
    moldCompound["Epoxy Molding Compound\nEMC black resin"]
    pcbPad["PCB Copper Pads\nwith solder paste"]
    solderJoint["Solder Joint\nSAC305 Sn-Ag-Cu"]

    siliconDie --> dieAttach
    dieAttach --> paddle
    siliconDie --> bondWires
    bondWires --> innerLeads
    innerLeads --> leadFrame
    leadFrame --> outerLeads
    paddle --> moldCompound
    bondWires --> moldCompound
    siliconDie --> moldCompound
    outerLeads --> pcbPad
    pcbPad --> solderJoint
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Heat and electrical signals flow **vertically** from the die, through the bond wires to the lead frame, and out through the gull-wing leads to the PCB. The molding compound encapsulates the die, bond wires, and inner lead frame — everything except the outer leads.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — BGA Cross-Section and Signal Path

```mermaid
graph TD
    bgaDie["Silicon Die\nflip-chip attached"]
    bgaSubstrate["Package Substrate\nBT resin or build-up Ajinomoto"]
    bgaUnderfill["Underfill Material\nepoxy capillary flow"]
    bgaBalls["Solder Balls\nSAC305 diameter 0.4 to 0.76 mm"]
    bgaPcbPad["PCB Pad with NSMD\nnonsolder mask defined"]
    bgaHeatSpreader["Heat Spreader\ncopper or aluminum lid"]
    bgaTim["Thermal Interface Material\nTIM1 between die and lid"]

    bgaDie --> bgaSubstrate
    bgaDie --> bgaTim
    bgaTim --> bgaHeatSpreader
    bgaSubstrate --> bgaBalls
    bgaBalls --> bgaPcbPad
    bgaDie --> bgaUnderfill
    bgaUnderfill --> bgaSubstrate
```

> **Reading the diagram:** BGA is **structurally different** from QFP. Instead of bond wires on the periphery, the die is **flip-chip soldered** directly to a multilayer organic substrate. Solder balls connect the substrate to the PCB. Heat exits **upward** through the heat spreader (with TIM) — a much better thermal path than QFP.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Thermal Resistance Network (Electrical Analogy)

The following diagram shows the **thermal circuit equivalent** of the die-to-ambient heat flow path. The student can use this to draw the **thermal equivalent circuit** in an exam.

```mermaid
graph LR
    tJ["TJ\nJunction Temp"] --- rJC["thetaJC\nJunction to Case"]
    rJC --- tC["TC\nCase Temp"]
    tC --- rCS["thetaCS\nCase to Heatsink"]
    rCS --- tS["TS\nHeatsink Temp"]
    tS --- rSA["thetaSA\nHeatsink to Ambient"]
    rSA --- tA["TA\nAmbient Temp"]
    pD["PD\nPower Dissipated"] --- rJC
    pD --- tJ
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is the **electrical analog** of a series voltage divider with a current source $P_D$. Just as a current source injects current through series resistors creating voltage drops, the power $P_D$ flows through series thermal resistances creating temperature drops. The student should label $T_J$ as the highest potential, $T_A$ as the lowest (ground).
